title: Design history: ideaflow-vision wiki — scaffolding decisions
description:
Documentation of design decisions for the new `ideaflow-vision` wiki at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision.

## Origin
Jacob asked to synthesize ~57 Slack messages tagged #explainingideaflow (2022→Feb 2026) plus future notestream captures into a coherent LLM wiki about the IdeaFlow vision. Separate from vision-convo (which is one conversation's snapshot).

## Claude's original proposal
- Name: `ideaflow-vision`
- Structure: index.md, AGENTS.md, raw/ by year-window, wiki/framings/ (one page per metaphor), wiki/pitches/ (audience-specific), wiki/themes/, wiki/sources/
- Synthesize Jacob's own framings, cross-link to vision-convo
- Skip notestream for v1

## Codex second opinion (gpt-5.4 high, session 019daa5f)
- ✅ Keep separate wiki (agreed)
- ⚠️ "Too axis-heavy" — don't make framing, audience, theme, era all first-class
- Recommended hybrid: primary=functional layers (personal/team/humanity), secondary=framings, single chronology.md, single entry-points.md
- 80–90% synthesis, 10–20% verbatim quotes
- Add page types: north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, chronology, canonical-terms, open-questions, use-cases
- Rename `pitches/` to `translations/` or `entry-points/` or `explainers/` — "pitches" reads salesy
- Risks: context collapse, internal language leakage, metaphor sprawl, product/worldview blur

## Jacob's decisions
1. **Keep `pitches/`** — overrule Codex's rename. Pitches is good.
2. **Use verbatim quotes heavily** — keep original lines, don't over-synthesize. Jacob's voice matters.
3. **Framing for evolution:** "The vision hasn't necessarily changed longitudinally, but it has been refined and has evolved and matured, and more forks have been discovered, with more details and realizations." → this is the backbone of chronology.md
4. Adopt Codex's missing page types (north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, etc.)
5. Adopt Codex's functional-layer primary organization (personal → team → humanity)
6. Save this design history in the wiki itself (wiki/meta/design-history.md) and in this beads ticket

## Risk mitigations adopted
- Editorial note in AGENTS.md acknowledging synthesis of historical framings
- Date every ingested excerpt
- Separate canonical current framing from historical
- Link from framings/ pages back to raw/ with timestamps

## Related
- Vision-convo wiki: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/vision-convo
- Slack source archive (local-only): ~/code/ideaflow-slack-wiki/
- Original session: 1137ed19-6d0a-4294-9240-7c559603a947 (line 462)
- Codex session: 019daa5f-4a5c-7771-a654-a07d44510344

title: Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md with reserved subdomain list
description:
Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md instead of wikihub.globalbr.ai/@user/slug.

## Product questions to resolve
1. What counts as a user's 'personal wiki'? Specific slug, default wiki, or first-created?
2. Do non-personal wikis get sub-subdomains (recipes.jacobcole.wikihub.md) or stay path-based?
3. How does @wikihub community index behave? (wikihub.wikihub.md is awkward)

## Required
- **Reserved subdomain list**: www, api, collab, staging, app, admin, dev, explore, help, docs, blog, about, status, community, wiki, wikihub (and more — architect will propose full list)
- **Wildcard DNS** (*.wikihub.md) — Cloudflare
- **Wildcard SSL cert** — Let's Encrypt DNS-01 via Cloudflare API, or Cloudflare Universal SSL
- **Nginx wildcard server block** on AWS Lightsail origin
- **Flask subdomain routing** — SERVER_NAME-based or Host header middleware
- **Username collision migration** — check existing usernames against reserved list
- **Backward compat** — /@user/slug URLs keep working (301 redirect to canonical subdomain)

## Interacts with
- wikihub-w0s4 (collab editor) — subdomain-hosted pages need to connect to wss://api.wikihub.md/collab; CORS, CSP, cookie scope concerns.
- wikihub-dz5 (community index wiki @wikihub) — conflict resolution needed

## Architect design doc
Pending — feature-dev:code-architect agent in progress.

title: ADR: markdown-as-source vs DB-as-source for feed/likes — granularity decision
description:
Architecture note for the feed/follow/likes epic (wikihub-9bi, wikihub-6b1).

The feed itself does NOT force an architecture change. Only the *granularity of likes* (and other per-item interactions) does. Three levels:

## Level 1: Coarse-grained (page / commit / wiki) — RECOMMENDED FOR v1
- `likes` table in Postgres with FK to `page_id` or commit hash
- Markdown stays untouched, git stays authoritative (invariant preserved)
- Activity feed pulls from git log + Postgres
- Diff-likes are actually the cleanest unit for a feed — a feed entry *is* a diff
- Ships in days, no architectural debt

## Level 2: Per-line / per-entry — deferred
Three sub-options if we ever need this:
- **Obsidian-style block IDs** (`^abc123` trailing a line). Markdown-native, git-diffable, human-readable. Cheapest middle path. Used by Obsidian, Bear, Logseq.
- **Invisible HTML comment anchors** (`<!-- id:abc -->`). Same idea, uglier.
- **Yegge flip: DB-as-source, markdown-as-export.** Biggest rewrite — `git_sync.py`, hooks, and the "git is source of truth" invariant all reverse. Only justified if structured data (tables, transclusion, typed fields) becomes dominant.

## Decision
Ship Level 1 now (likes on pages + commits/diffs). If per-entry demand emerges, add Obsidian block IDs — additive, doesn't break the invariant, keeps the Yegge flip as optional future work rather than forced.

## Trigger for revisiting
Revisit the Yegge flip when any of these is true:
- Per-entry interactions become the primary mode of engagement
- Structured/typed fields (article-type system wikihub-pua, infobox wikihub-60v) dominate content
- Real-time collab (wikihub-ibt.6) needs operational transforms/CRDTs that markdown files can't express

## References
- Blocks: wikihub-9bi (Follow users + personalized feed)
- Blocks: wikihub-6b1 (Per-user activity feeds)
- Related: wikihub-pua (article type system), wikihub-60v (infobox frontmatter), wikihub-ibt.6 (real-time collab)
- External: Steve Yegge's writing on DB-as-source with flat-file export

title: Password manager handoff: trigger native PM save after agent signup
description:
## Problem

When an agent signs up for wikihub via `POST /api/v1/accounts`, the returned credentials (username, password, api_key) never flow into the user's password manager the way a normal browser signup would. Native PMs (Chrome/Safari built-in, 1Password extension) trigger on successful form-based login events — not on API calls from an agent. So the user is left with:

- No saved login for future human access
- No saved API key for future agent sessions (which lose context on compaction)
- Nothing discoverable in their PM by site-search later

## Key technical unlock

**`navigator.credentials.store(new PasswordCredential({id, password}))`** — a web standard designed for exactly this. Chrome and Safari's built-in PMs honor it; 1Password/Bitwarden browser extensions typically catch it too. **No extension or PM setup required** from the user.

## Proposed flow (default path)

1. `POST /api/v1/accounts` → already returns `{username, password, api_key}`. Add a `claim_url` (short-lived, single-use token like magic-link) to the response.
2. Agent either opens `claim_url` itself (if it has browser control) or hands it to the user: "open this in your browser to save to your password manager."
3. New route `/claim?token=…`:
   - Consumes one-time token, auto-logs user in (same mechanism as magic-link)
   - Calls `navigator.credentials.store(new PasswordCredential({id: username, password}))` → PM save prompt
   - Displays the API key visibly with a copy button + `onepassword://addItem?...` deep link for users with 1Password
   - Fallback for old browsers: a real `<form method="POST" action="/login">` with `autocomplete="username"` / `autocomplete="new-password"` that the user submits once — native "save password?" prompt fires on successful login

## Dimensions that matter (the right answer branches on these)

- **Agent runtime**: browser-driving agent (can complete handoff itself) vs. CLI like Claude Code (must hand a URL to a human)
- **Credential shape**: password (for future human login), API key (for agent re-auth), magic link (one-shot). All three useful, PMs handle differently
- **Destination**: browser PM / OS keychain / PM app / clipboard — each needs a different mechanism
- **User's PM setup**: 1Password CLI installed? extension only? nothing at all? Claim page should progressively enhance

## Ranked options (best-default → edge-case)

1. **Credential Management API on a claim page** — best default, no extension needed
2. **Autosubmit a real login form** — dumb/universal, triggers built-in "save password?" prompt
3. **PM deep links** (`onepassword://addItem`, `bitwarden://...`) — powerful for known-PM users, brittle as default
4. **CLI integration** (`op item create`) — for CLI agents where user's PM CLI is detectable
5. **QR code / email fallback** — universal escape hatch when all else fails

## Cheapest validation first

Before building anything: open a scratch HTML page that calls `navigator.credentials.store()` with a fake credential, in Jacob's actual browser with his actual PM setup. See what prompt appears. That one experiment tells us whether the "no extension needed" story holds for real users.

## Related surfaces to update if built

- `app/routes/api.py` — `POST /api/v1/accounts` response adds `claim_url`
- New route `/claim` (probably in `auth` blueprint)
- `AGENTS.md` — document the claim flow for agents
- `llms.txt` / `/AGENTS.md` / landing page — mention "credentials will be saved to your browser's password manager automatically"
- Agent surfaces sync list in `AGENTS.md` under "agent instruction surfaces"

## Context

Brainstormed in session on 2026-04-17. User asked: "Could we trigger a password manager to save the API key and username somehow even if someone doesn't have 1Password installed?" Answer: yes, via Credential Management API on a claim page.

title: Support preparing collaborator invites without sending notification emails yet
description:
Follow-up from the team-wiki prep task (`wikihub-vdwe`).

Current behavior:
- bulk/wiki/page share immediately creates pending invites and sends notification emails

Need:
- a way to stage collaborator/invite preparation without actually notifying people yet
- examples: draft the invite roster, preview the payload, or create pending invite records with email sending suppressed until an explicit later action

Why:
This blocks the founder workflow of preparing a team wiki and collaborators ahead of time, then reviewing before the first send.

Open questions:
- should this be a dry-run mode only?
- or true staged pending invites with a later 'send now' action?
- how should the UI/API expose draft vs sent state?

title: Optimistic locking (If-Match / ETag / 409)

description: multiple dropdown menus across the site use hover to show/hide, but there's a gap or timing issue that causes the dropdown to disappear before the user can reach it. known instances: (1) download/clone dropdown on wiki pages — hover the download icon, try to move mouse down to the options, dropdown vanishes. (2) account menu in top-right nav — same issue. (3) hamburger menu on mobile. audit ALL hover-based dropdowns site-wide and fix them all. likely fix: add a small padding/bridge between trigger and dropdown, or switch to click-to-toggle with click-outside-to-close.

This ticket's original scope was a generic app/email.py with send_transactional(). Proposal: let wikihub-exj1 own provider selection + base send helper; scope this ticket down to only the transactional-auth-specific templates (verify, reset, account-notification) once that infra lands. Alternatively, close this and fold its content into exj1's "Infra first" section. Decision needed before starting. See scratch/auth_design.md §B.

title: Admin toggle to enable/disable Curator chat
description:
Add an admin setting to enable/disable the Curator chat feature, controllable both programmatically and via the admin UI.

## Programmatic
- Env var: CURATOR_ENABLED=true/false (default: false)
- Also stored in DB (admin_settings table or similar) so it can be toggled without restart
- DB setting overrides env var if set

## Admin UI
- Add toggle on the settings/admin page
- Simple on/off switch with label "Curator Agent"
- When off: chat panel doesn't render, /api/v1/agent/chat returns 503
- When on: panel renders on reader pages for authenticated users

## Implementation
- Add CURATOR_ENABLED to app config (read from env + DB)
- Add admin API endpoint: POST /api/v1/admin/settings {curator_enabled: bool}
- Add toggle to admin panel UI (settings.html or admin page)
- Guard the chat endpoint: return 503 if disabled
- Guard the frontend: only render panel HTML if enabled
- Guard by auth: only show to logged-in users (not anonymous visitors)

title: URL / repo connect (import from public git repos)
description: git clone --bare + walk tree + index into Postgres. Plumbing exists in git_sync.py. Need UI form and background worker.

title: Beads: add reviewed/unreviewed status for human review tracking
Landing page: 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' CTA
description:
Consider adding secondary CTAs on the landing page hero alongside 'Publish a wiki in seconds'. Candidates:

1. **'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity'** — the grand vision, taps into Snow Crash / Librarian philosophy. wikihub isn't just a tool, it's a collective knowledge project.

2. **'Make private collaborative lists with agents'** — the practical agent use case. Not everything is public publishing — private wikis where your agent writes and organizes alongside you.

3. **'Agent-native Google Drive'** — positions wikihub as the file system / knowledge layer that agents can actually read and write to, unlike Google Drive. Markdown files in git = structured, versionable, API-accessible. Google Drive is for humans with browsers; wikihub is for humans AND agents. This framing resonates with the 'why not just use Notion/Google Docs' objection — those tools have terrible agent APIs.

These could be secondary CTAs, tagline variants, rotating hero text, or value prop cards below the fold. The key insight: wikihub has three distinct value props for three audiences:
- **Publishers** → 'Publish a wiki in seconds' (Karpathy wave, Obsidian users)
- **Visionaries** → 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' (knowledge builders, researchers)
- **Agent users** → 'Agent-native Google Drive' or 'Collaborate with agents on private knowledge' (developers, AI power users)

title: Restructure Jeremy Nixon wiki into Karpathy three-layer format
description:
Jeremy's 452 pages are currently flat in the wiki root. The Karpathy pattern organizes into:

- schema.md — what this wiki is about, how it's structured
- index.md — table of contents (already exists)
- log.md — timeline of additions
- wiki/ — compiled, curated knowledge pages by topic
- raw/ — unprocessed source entries (the current 450 dated posts)

An agent could:
1. Move all dated entries into raw/
2. Create wiki/ pages that compile the best insights by topic (machine intelligence, learning, startups, thinking frameworks, etc.)
3. Generate schema.md describing the wiki structure
4. Create log.md with a timeline

This is a good showcase of the 'agent maintains a wiki' use case — the raw material exists, an LLM compiles it into structured knowledge.

title: MCP connector (standalone Python) + Claude-session-auto-wiki hook
description:
Build a standalone Python MCP server at ~/code/wikihub/mcp-server/ that mirrors the noos/mcp-server/ pattern. Replaces the hand-rolled Flask /mcp endpoint (app/routes/agent_surfaces.py:539-570) with a proper @modelcontextprotocol/sdk-compatible server.

Full design blueprint: docs/MCP_CONNECTOR_BLUEPRINT.md (written 2026-04-22 by a research subagent that mapped the codebase).

## Headline value props

1. Auto-build an LLM wiki from Claude chat history (analogous to noos portable-context-ingest skill, adapted to WikiHub page model)
2. Live SessionEnd hook — after each Claude session, a two-stage evaluator (YES/NO cheap filter → synthesis) decides whether to write a new wiki page

## Tool surface (10 tools)

Read: wikihub_search, wikihub_get_page, wikihub_list_pages
Write: wikihub_create_page, wikihub_update_page, wikihub_append_section, wikihub_delete_page
Meta: wikihub_list_wikis, wikihub_register_agent
Unique: wikihub_curate (wraps /agent/chat SSE → returns final text)

## Stages

- MVP: 9 tools + Starlette HTTP + systemd unit on Lightsail 54.145.123.7 + Cloudflare DNS mcp.wikihub.md
- v1.1: ~/.claude/hooks/wikihub-session-end.sh + SessionEnd hook entry
- v2: wikihub_curate SSE wrapper + portable-context-ingest skill split (facts→noos, narratives→wikihub) + post-write noos_relate cross-link

## Risks

- Cloudflare requires User-Agent: curl/8.0 (default in api.py layer)
- wikihub_curate nests SSE-in-SSE; test that path early
- Per-user Anthropic key required for /agent/chat (or server env)

## Sibling tickets

- noos-m5pt — duplicate in noos beads (close one once the work lives in the canonical repo; this one in wikihub beads is the canonical home)
- noos-q2e2 — portable-context-ingest skill (reusable synthesis logic)
- noos-uh2u — Phase 2 full chat export (similar ingestion mechanics)

title: Design GitHub sync and pushback for repo-backed wikis
description: Design a longer-term model for keeping a WikiHub wiki automatically up to date from a linked GitHub repository and, potentially, pushing structured edits back to GitHub. Scope: one-way sync from GitHub to WikiHub, bidirectional update semantics, conflict handling, branch/PR model vs direct push, audit history, and how repo-backed wikis differ from ordinary wikis. This should stay separate from the initial public-repo onboarding import ticket because it needs deeper product and source-of-truth design.

The heading text itself renders correctly. The HTML source confirms the TOC links contain double-escaped entities.

title: Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[@owner/slug/path]] syntax with hover previews
description:
Support `[[wikilinks]]` across wikis, not just within one wiki. Today `[[Page Title]]` resolves within the current wiki; cross-wiki links require full URLs (`https://wikihub.md/@owner/slug/path`) which skip the rich hover-preview treatment.

## Why it matters

1. **Hover previews across wikis.** WikiHub's hover-preview cards only fire for internal wikilinks. Users crossing between wikis (e.g., ideaflow-vision → vision-convo) lose the preview UX and get raw anchor tags instead.
2. **Rename resilience.** Full URLs are brittle: rename a page and every outbound link from a sister wiki breaks silently. Cross-wiki wikilinks (resolved against canonical IDs or current titles) survive renames.
3. **Unified backlink graph.** The `wikilinks` table / page-graph today stops at wiki boundaries. Cross-wiki wikilinks would make a user's multi-wiki network legible as one graph — which is exactly the worldview WikiHub is hosting.
4. **Authoring ergonomics.** Writing `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow]]` beats copy-pasting long URLs, especially in LLM-authored content.

## Triggering example

Just scaffolded `/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision` with 15+ cross-links to `/@jacobcole/vision-convo`. All use full `https://wikihub.md/...` URLs. None trigger hover previews. If vision-convo ever renames `projects/ideaflow.md`, every outbound link from ideaflow-vision breaks.

## Proposed syntax (pick one — need decision)

1. **Path-qualified:** `[[@owner/slug/path/to/page]]` — explicit, unambiguous, closest to the URL shape. Display text defaults to page title; pipe for alias: `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow|IdeaFlow]]`.
2. **Prefix-qualified:** `[[wiki:owner/slug:Page Title]]` — MediaWiki-style interwiki prefix. Less URL-like, more title-driven.
3. **Scheme-prefixed:** `[[wh://owner/slug/path]]` — most future-proof if we ever want other schemes (e.g., `gh://` for GitHub).

Recommend option 1 — closest to what users already see in URLs.

## Semantics to decide

- **Title vs path resolution.** Do we match the target wiki's page by path (stable, URL-like) or by title (friendlier, changes with renames)?
- **Permission handling.** If the target page is private, the link should render but hover preview should 404 gracefully (not leak metadata).
- **Rendering fallback.** If the target wiki doesn't exist or the page isn't found, render as a broken-wikilink (`color-wikilink-broken`) rather than a raw URL.
- **Backlink visibility.** Should `/@vision-convo/projects/ideaflow` see its inbound cross-wiki links from `/@ideaflow-vision/*` in its backlinks panel?

## Implementation sketch

Touch points:
- `app/renderer.py` — markdown-it-py wikilink plugin needs to recognize the cross-wiki pattern, build the correct URL, and mark the link for hover preview.
- `app/models.py` — `wikilinks` table currently scoped to one wiki; needs nullable `target_wiki_id` to represent cross-wiki edges.
- Hover preview fetcher in `app/templates/base.html` — currently hits `/api/v1/wikis/<current>/pages/...`; needs to honor a data attribute or URL pattern to switch wiki.
- Backlinks query — join across wikis when target page has cross-wiki backrefs.

## Out of scope (v1)

- Cross-account renames (what if target user renames their account).
- Private-to-private resolution (requires share-grant check at render time).
- Drag-and-drop authoring UI.

## Related

- Scaffolded the two-wiki setup that surfaced this: `ideaflow-vision` ↔ `vision-convo` on 2026-04-20. See `wikihub-4i3b` (closed).
- Vision-convo's `themes/llm-wiki-as-medium.md` already notes WikiHub as the IDE — cross-wiki linking is part of that IDE story.

## Priority justification

P2 — not blocking current work (full URLs work), but every day we don't have this, the cross-wiki graph gets staler and links get more fragile. Becomes P1 when a third wiki joins the network or a cross-wiki rename breaks something visibly.

title: WikiHub PKM client app (desktop + mobile)
description:
## Vision

Build a dedicated client app for WikiHub that turns it into a full personal knowledge management (PKM) system — like Obsidian but backed by WikiHub's hosted git repos.

## Platforms

- Desktop: Electron app (or native Swift for Mac)
- Mobile: iOS / Android
- Web: could be the first step (online editor/reader)

## Key Questions to Decide

1. **Build from scratch vs. extend existing?**
   - Build from scratch — most control, cleanest WikiHub integration, but most work
   - Fork/extend an open-source markdown editor (e.g., Marktext, Nota, Obsidian-like)
   - Integrate with Notestream (IdeaFlow's existing app)
   - Wrap the WikiHub web UI in Electron as a starting point

2. **Offline-first architecture?**
   - Local git clone of wiki repos + sync on connect
   - Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins? merge?)
   - How much works without internet?

3. **What differentiates this from Obsidian?**
   - WikiHub-native: publish, fork, star, discover — social PKM
   - Agent-native: MCP server means your AI assistant can maintain your wiki
   - Karpathy pattern built in: raw/, wiki/, schema structure
   - No plugin ecosystem needed — opinionated defaults

4. **MVP scope**
   - Online web editor might be the fastest first step
   - WikiHub already renders markdown — adding an edit mode could be v0.1
   - Desktop app (Electron) as v0.2
   - Mobile as v0.3

## Why

Using WikiHub as a PKM system requires a better editing experience than the current web form. A dedicated client would make it viable for daily use — quick capture, wikilink navigation, offline editing, keyboard shortcuts, graph view, etc.

title: Right-hand sidebar (TOC, graph) disappeared — regression
Sidebar folders collapse when navigating via sidebar link — state not persisted on page load
description:
## Problem

When clicking a sidebar link to navigate to a new page, folders that were expanded on the previous page collapse on the new page. User loses their place in the tree each time they navigate.

## Root cause

`applyFolderState()` in `app/templates/reader.html` (around line 918) only respects folder open/closed state if the user explicitly clicked the toggle chevron (which writes to `localStorage['wikihub-sidebar-folders']`).

Folders that auto-expand because they contain the active page are opened server-side via the template's `{{ 'open' if item.active else '' }}` — this state is never written to localStorage. On the next page load (in a different folder), that folder's entry in localStorage is still missing, so it falls through to "non-active folders with no localStorage state stay collapsed".

Effect: every time you click through to a page in a different branch of the tree, the previously-active folder collapses.

## Suggested fix (NOT a major refactor — ~15 lines of JS)

In `applyFolderState()`, after deciding the state for each folder, write the final state back to localStorage. That way any folder that was open on page load (whether by explicit toggle or by auto-expand for active page) persists its openness through navigation.

Edge case to preserve: if the user explicitly collapsed a folder (`state[path] === false`), keep that intent. Only persist `true` for folders that end up open.

## Files

- `app/templates/reader.html` — `applyFolderState()` near line 918
- Same pattern exists in `app/templates/folder.html` and `app/templates/profile.html` — keep consistent
- Tag as `recurring` once fixed since sidebar JS is historically fragile (see wikihub-58c, wikihub-bnj, wikihub-9c8j)

## Related

- wikihub-a6l (closed): established auto-expand-to-active behavior
- wikihub-9c8j (open): sidebar reorders items on click (different bug, similar area)

## Verification

- Open a wiki, click a page deep in folder A. Confirm folder A expands.
- Click a page deep in folder B. Folder A should remain expanded. Currently it collapses.

title: Design third-party collaboration flow: suggestions, comments, and eventual collaborator push
description:
# Third-party collaboration flow — design options

**Status: DRAFT — needs human review before implementation.**
Created 2026-04-20 from a product-planning session (Claude + Codex second opinion).

## Context

Today `public-edit` allows anonymous writes via web UI / API, but git push still requires owner auth (`app/git_backend.py` receive_pack gated on owner). User wants third-party collab:
- Request edit/comment access
- Comment on pages (bottom thread + inline/line-level)
- Suggest changes
- Eventually: git push for collaborators

## Option A — Fork-based PR flow (forge-lite)

Build a ~simpler GitHub review UI on top of git primitives.
- `PullRequest` model tying two branches (source fork → target authoritative)
- Threaded comments anchored by `(blob_sha, line_number)`
- Merge button → `git merge --no-ff`
- Requires: fork mirror repos, branch UI, merge/rebase UX

**Pros:** natural upgrade for multi-page restructures, long-term contributors, agents pushing via git.
**Cons:** heavy user mental model (branches/forks), lots of UI to build, wrong audience (WikiHub is prose-first, not code-first).

## Option B — Lightweight proposal review (no fork)

Wikipedia "Pending Changes" / Google Docs "Suggesting" style. No forks, no branches exposed.

**Schema (Codex refinement — not just one flat table):**
- `Proposal` — the durable review object (status, author, title, comment)
- `ProposalRevision` — lets suggester iterate after reviewer feedback (critical: without this, the object is disposable and non-conversational)
- `ProposalPagePatch` — one or more page patches per revision. UI exposes single-page suggestions first, but schema supports multi-page from day one (cheap now, expensive later)
- `CommentThread` — page-level (bottom) and inline anchors, attached to proposals OR pages directly

**Line-comment anchoring (prose-specific):**
- Hard anchor: `(page_id, blob_sha, line_number)` for audit/history
- Soft anchor: quoted text + short prefix/suffix context
- On re-render, try fuzzy relocation; if confidence low, mark outdated + collapse (GitHub style)
- **Do NOT do semantic anchoring** — sounds smart, becomes debugging sludge

**Owner review queue:** accept / reject / request-changes. Accept = app writes the diff as a normal commit (suggester as author, owner as merger). If `base_content_hash != HEAD`, show rebase/resubmit UI — no auto-merge heroics.

**Steal from existing products:**
- Wikipedia: Pending Changes for untrusted contributors on high-signal wikis
- Wikipedia: "Request edit access" as a lightweight trust workflow
- Notion: inline suggestion mode with accept/reject
- Google Docs: Suggesting mode
- GitHub: outdated-comment collapsing

## Option C — GitHub federation (escape hatch only)

Host on GitHub, mirror into WikiHub, "comment on GitHub" button. Not the core collaboration model — export path for power users.

## Option D — CRDTs / real-time collab

Wrong problem. This is async review, trust, and moderation, not multiplayer typing. See `wikihub-w0s4` (real-time editor MVP) — that's a different product surface.

## Recommendation (pending review)

**Ship B first.** But NOT as "one flat SuggestedEdit row forever" — model as Proposal + ProposalRevision + ProposalPagePatch from day one, even if UI only exposes single-page suggestions at first.

**MVP entities:**
- Proposal
- ProposalRevision
- ProposalPagePatch (UI: single page)
- CommentThread (page + inline anchors)
- Owner review queue (accept/reject/request-changes)
- Conflict handling: rebase/resubmit UI when base hash changed
- "Request edit access" button → lightweight trust grant

**Defer:**
- A (fork-based PRs) → only when there's real demand for multi-page proposals or long-term external contributors
- Collaborator git push (wikihub-uyi) → only after roles + audit + moderation norms exist
- CRDTs / real-time collab → separate product surface

## Relationship to existing tickets

- `wikihub-ub2.2` (P1) — Comments on pages and wikis. Subsumed by CommentThread above.
- `wikihub-ibt.4` (P2) — Inline diff UI for suggested edits. Subsumed / superseded by Proposal flow.
- `wikihub-uyi` (P2) — Git remote add UI + collaborator push. Part 1 (owner "add remote" UI) independent; Part 2 (collaborator push) deferred behind this design.
- `wikihub-w0s4` (P2) — Real-time collab editor. Separate surface, not a replacement.

## Failure modes to handle

1. **Non-conversational review** — reviewer asks for changes, suggester can't respond cleanly. Solved by ProposalRevision.
2. **Stale base** — page changed after proposal submitted. Solved by rebase/resubmit prompt, not auto-merge.
3. **Multi-page coherence** — restructure spanning several pages as one unit. Solved by Proposal → multiple ProposalPagePatch from the schema start.
4. **Outdated line comments** — prose gets rewritten, anchors drift. Solved by fuzzy context anchor + outdated collapsing.
5. **Partial accept** — owner wants some hunks, not others. Defer, but schema leaves room.
6. **Abuse / trust** — unlimited anonymous proposals become spam. "Request edit access" + owner moderation queue + per-user rate limits.

## Next step

Human review of this plan. If approved, break MVP entities into implementation tickets with dependencies.

title: Graph system: page graph, full graph, filtered graph with progressive coloring
description:
## Context

Wikis are graphs. The wikilink structure already contains rich relationship data — we just need to visualize it. This spec defines a three-view graph system that works on any wiki (structure-agnostic) but looks better with metadata (progressive enhancement).

## Three views, one graph engine

Underlying data: nodes = pages, edges = wikilinks. Derived from the wikilinks index wikihub already builds for backlinks. No separate graph database needed.

### 1. Page graph (every page sidebar/footer)
- Current page = center node, highlighted
- Show all pages directly linked TO or FROM this page (1-hop)
- Optionally expand to 2-hop (friends of friends)
- Click node → navigate to that page
- Like Obsidian's local graph — scoped, useful, not overwhelming
- Must be fast enough to render inline on every page load

### 2. Full graph (wiki homepage + dedicated route)
- Every page is a node, every wikilink is an edge
- Force-directed layout — clusters emerge naturally from link density
- The "wow" view showing the shape of the whole knowledge base
- Zoom, pan, search, click-to-navigate
- Shown on wiki homepage (index.md) as an interactive embed and at a dedicated `/@user/wiki/graph` route

### 3. Filtered graph (overlays on full graph)
- Filter by: directory path, frontmatter `type:`, tag, or search query
- "Show only pages connected to X" — page graph launched from full graph
- "Hide all conversation pages" — reduce noise to see topic structure
- Toggle filters on/off, combine them

## Coloring strategy (progressive enhancement)

The graph looks good with zero schema knowledge and great with it:

| Signal | How to use | Agnostic? |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Directory path | `wiki/courses/` = blue, `wiki/topics/` = green, etc. | Yes — any wiki with folders |
| Frontmatter `type:` | Override directory color if present, more precise | Semi — only typed wikis |
| Frontmatter `tags:` | Secondary color ring or icon badge | Semi |
| Link count | Node size scales with inbound + outbound links | Yes — derived from wikilinks |
| Recency | Opacity or glow based on `updated:` or git date | Semi |

**Fallback:** no directories, no frontmatter, no nothing → every node same color, sized by link count. Still works. Degrades gracefully.

**Key rule:** never require metadata for the graph to function. Only use it to enhance when present.

## Where graphs appear

- **Page sidebar/footer**: page graph (1-hop), always visible, lightweight
- **Wiki homepage (index.md)**: full graph embedded inline — interactive `<canvas>` or collapsible section. WikiHub auto-injects on index.md pages, or expose a `{{graph}}` embed directive
- **Dedicated `/graph` route**: full-screen interactive graph with filter controls, per wiki

The homepage embed doesn't violate structure-agnosticism — it's a rendering feature of the homepage route, not imposed on content.

## Tech notes

- **Data source:** wikilinks index (already exists for backlinks). Endpoints: `GET /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph` returning `{nodes: [{id, title, type?, dir?}], edges: [{source, target}]}`
- **Frontend:** d3-force or similar force-directed layout. Lightweight, browser-native.
- **Performance:** wikis under 500 pages — render all nodes. Above that — progressive loading or cluster-first-then-expand. Page graph (1-hop) must be instant.
- **Interaction:** hover = show title + link count, click = navigate, drag = reposition, scroll = zoom

title: Rename public wiki `vision-convo` to `civilizational-vision`
description:
Rename the public wiki currently at `@jacobcole/vision-convo` to `@jacobcole/civilizational-vision` soon.

Why:
- `vision-convo` describes the provenance (a conversation) rather than the substance.
- As a public-facing long-term wiki, the current name sounds archival/process-oriented instead of like a coherent worldview or onboarding destination.
- `civilizational-vision` better matches the content: coordination, collective intelligence, human flourishing, world-building, and system-level transformation.

Desired outcome:
- The wiki's public identity shifts from "notes from a conversation" to "a structured body of ideas about civilization-scale vision."
- Source provenance should still be preserved inside `raw/` and/or an editorial note on the homepage.

Implementation notes:
- Rename the wiki slug if supported cleanly.
- If direct rename is awkward, create the new wiki and migrate content.
- Preserve existing inbound links if possible (redirect preferred).
- Update any internal references from `vision-convo` to `civilizational-vision` where appropriate.
- Keep the sister relationship with the future `ideaflow-vision` wiki explicit.

Acceptance:
- A concrete plan exists to move the public wiki from `vision-convo` to `civilizational-vision` without losing the provenance context, and the rename/migration is scheduled as a near-term cleanup.

title: Inline expansion of own wikis in left sidebar (no navigation needed)
description:
## Goal
In the left-hand sidebar on a user's profile page (e.g. /@jacobcole), allow expanding the tree of a wiki inline to see its pages, without having to first navigate into the wiki.

## Current behavior
Sidebar shows top-level wikis grouped by folder (health/, ideas/, local/, etc.) and a flat list of featured wikis. To see pages inside a wiki you have to click into it.

## Proposed behavior
Click a disclosure chevron next to a wiki name to expand it in place, showing the same page tree that would appear in the wiki's own sidebar (respecting ACLs / visibility). Collapsing returns to the compact view. State persists in localStorage so the tree stays as you left it across visits.

## Open questions / design considerations
- Scale: some wikis have hundreds of pages. Lazy-load page tree on first expand; cap initial depth; show "view all" affordance.
- Confusion risk: mixing wiki-level nav and page-level nav in one tree could muddle mental model. Maybe render expanded children indented with a subtle background so they read as "drilled in" rather than siblings of wikis.
- ACL: only show pages the current viewer can see (same rules as the wiki's own sidebar).
- Mobile: sidebar is already cramped; may need to stay collapsed-by-default on narrow viewports.
- Performance: fetch via the same endpoint used by the wiki's own sidebar; cache per-session.

## V2 scope
This is likely a V2 polish, not a V1 requirement — useful for owners navigating their own knowledge faster, and for visitors skimming a profile. Worth prototyping behind a feature flag to see if it's clarifying or overwhelming.

## Motivation
User (Jacob) has 10+ wikis and finds navigating into each one to see contents slow. Inline expansion would turn the profile into a live directory of his whole public knowledge base.

title: Noos design question (reference): heavier base concept vs. list-of-lists
description:
## Cross-project reference

This ticket exists on the WikiHub side for visibility; the actual design question lives on Noos.

## The question

Does Noos need a heavier unit above `list` — a "base" or "space" with distinct identity, visibility boundary, export surface, or collaborator set? Today Noos is uniform: nodes + edges, with `list` as the container type and hierarchy built from list-of-lists.

## Why WikiHub cares

WikiHub's "wiki" is exactly that heavier unit — a git repo with its own ACL, mirror, and identity. The contrast between the two systems is informative either way: if Noos adds a heavier unit, agent guidance needs to mirror WikiHub's "page-first" heuristic. If Noos stays flat, the guidance stays "list-freely."

## Link

Noos side of the discussion: will be pinned here once created.

Status: reference only. Track the decision on the Noos side; echo outcomes back to this ticket.

Currently wiki index pages are either a rendered index.md or a plain file listing. A Quartz-style index would automatically generate a richer overview with:

This could be opt-in via frontmatter (layout: quartz-index) or the default when no index.md exists.

title: Sidebar architecture: avoid full reload on page navigation
description:
Consider a larger navigation architecture change so the left sidebar does not fully reload when moving between pages.

Goal:
- preserve sidebar state and avoid rebuilding/re-fetching the entire sidebar tree on every page navigation

Possible directions to evaluate:
- partial page navigation / client-side router style transitions
- persistent layout shell with content-only swaps
- caching plus incremental sidebar updates keyed by wiki and current path
- stronger API contracts for sidebar diffs / active-path updates

This may require a substantial re-architecture of page navigation, rendering boundaries, and JS state ownership. Track it explicitly as architecture/design work before implementation.

title: Scrape Jeremy Nixon public Google Docs into wiki
DESIGN SPEC: one-command agent onboarding — verification is never a gate
description:
## The invariant

**`POST /api/v1/accounts` returns an API key that is immediately usable for every read/write operation on the caller's own data — without email verification, without browser interaction, without a second round-trip.**

This is the core value prop. It is the reason wikihub ships with 28 users on day 10. Any design change that violates it is wrong, regardless of how it's justified.

## What this forbids

Email verification MUST NOT gate any of these:
- Account creation (`POST /api/v1/accounts`)
- API key issuance / rotation
- Creating wikis (`POST /api/v1/wikis`)
- Creating, updating, deleting pages on your own wikis
- Creating API keys additional to the first
- Reading public content
- The CLI's `signup`, `login`, `new`, `write`, `read` subcommands
- Any of the agent-discovery surfaces (`/llms.txt`, `/llms-full.txt`, MCP tools)

## What verification MAY gate (enrichment, not core)

- Materializing pending invites addressed to an email (`wikihub-yjsv`)
- Auto-link of Google OAuth to an existing unverified email (`wikihub-ks5t.4`)
- Login-by-email convenience (as an alternative to login-by-username)
- Sender-reputation badges in the collaborator UI
- Public display of email as a contact ("contact this user")

## Discovered-in-session rationale

Jacob 2026-04-22: "One-command agent onboarding workflow. If you have to verify an email before you start using the app, that's lame."

## Stretch goal — 'vanilla LLM' onboarding

Today agents with any HTTP client can onboard:
```
curl -sX POST https://wikihub.md/api/v1/accounts \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"username":"my-agent"}'
```

Vanilla Claude.ai and ChatGPT's built-in `web_fetch` are GET-only — they can't issue that POST without additional tooling (Custom GPTs with Actions, Claude Agent Skills with code exec, etc.). **Nice-to-have follow-up**: file a ticket to explore a GET-based onboarding endpoint (e.g. `/onboard?username=X`) that a GET-only LLM could hit via an instruction in a copy-pasted URL. Weigh against spam/abuse risk.

## Cross-reference for future agents

Any ticket that touches authentication, verification, account creation, or API key issuance MUST cite this spec and affirm that it does not violate the invariant. Cross-link it from:
- `wikihub-yjsv` (invite token)
- `wikihub-ks5t.*` (auth audit family)
- `wikihub-750v`, `wikihub-3i9i`, `wikihub-um90` (the UX-polish trio)
- `wikihub-v8wa` (multi-account switcher)
- Any new auth/verification work

## Acceptance criteria for this spec ticket

1. This description is referenced by name from AGENTS.md as a guardrail
2. `/AGENTS.md` agent-facing route adds a 'Design invariants' section that states the no-verification-gate rule verbatim
3. Cross-references added as comments on every open auth-related ticket
4. `tests/test_e2e.py` has a `test_agent_onboarding_no_verification_gate` that creates an account with no email, then exercises every core write path on that key — regression guard.

title: Signup/login: fix 1Password autofill so credentials save with a usable identifier
description:
Observed 2026-04-22 during the wikihub-skp7 invite-flow test: when signing up via the invite link, 1Password offered to save the login but the saved entry had a blank username — it didn't know whether to treat the email input or the WikiHub username input as the login identifier.

## Root cause (likely)

`app/templates/auth/signup.html` has two inputs that could be the login ID:
- `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` (the WikiHub username)
- `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` (the account email)

With `autocomplete="email"` alone (not "username email") and `autocomplete="username"` on the other field, 1Password/Chrome autofill heuristics don't know which to save. Login (/auth/login) only has a username field — so the email-based save from signup can't be replayed on login anyway.

## Proposed fix

1. On signup, mark the email input `autocomplete="username email"` so password managers save email as the login identifier (users remember emails better than WikiHub usernames).
2. On /auth/login, accept login by email OR username (needs route change) and expose both inputs, or keep one input with `autocomplete="username"` that accepts either.
3. Test in 1Password + Chrome + Safari password managers: signup → save → visit /auth/login → autofill should work.

Same class of issue probably affects the "email forward to login" redirect flow from `auth.signup` when someone already has an account.

See also: wikihub-[last-sign-in-hint ticket] and wikihub-[username-prefill ticket] — all three are invite-flow UX polish.

title: Connection system: cross-referencing patterns that prevent noise
description:
## Context

Cross-references are the whole point of a wiki over flat notes. But random/spurious connections make it worse than no connections. This issue defines a connection system that maximizes signal and minimizes noise.

Tested on a 115-page wiki — the patterns below emerged from what worked and what didn't.

## The problem

Agents generating wiki pages tend to either:
- Add zero connections (each page is an island)
- Add random connections based on keyword overlap ("both mention 'learning'" → spurious link)

Both are bad. We need inline wikilinks woven naturally into prose — like Wikipedia, not like a "see also" appendix.

## How connections work: inline wikilinks in prose

Links are woven into the text naturally as the page is written:

> "Harrison worked at [[orbit-internship|Orbit]] where [[mark-khrapko|Mark Khrapko]] mentored him on [[hiring-and-teams|hiring philosophy]] and he developed the [[cnn-spectrogram-analysis|CNN spectrogram classifier]]"

No dedicated "connections" section. If a relationship matters, it belongs in the prose. If it's not worth mentioning in prose, it's not a real connection. Backlinks (auto-generated by renderer) handle the reverse direction.

### Rules for inline linking

- **Link on first mention only** — don't link the same page multiple times in one section
- **Use display text** — `[[slug|Natural Text]]` so prose reads naturally
- **Don't over-link** — link when it adds navigational value, not every noun
- **Don't self-link** — a page never links to itself
- **Conservative matching** — better to miss a link than create a wrong one. "Mark" alone is ambiguous; "Mark Khrapko" is not.

Agents creating wiki pages should include inline links AS THEY WRITE, not as a separate pass. The schema/CLAUDE.md should specify this behavior.

## Cross-section connection taxonomy

Agents should specifically look for these high-value connection types:
- **person ↔ topic**: conversation pages link to topics discussed with that person
- **course ↔ topic**: academic material links to personal exploration of same concepts
- **topic ↔ topic**: thematic overlap between different knowledge areas
- **experience ↔ reflection**: what happened links to what was learned from it

Cross-section connections are almost always more valuable than within-section.

## Quality rules (anti-spam)

**MUST:**
- Pass the test: "would a reader learn something by following this link?"
- Reference specific shared content, not just shared keywords

**SHOULD:**
- Every page should have at least 2 outbound inline links (prevents orphans)
- Inline links are uncapped but governed by "first mention, don't over-link"

**MUST NOT:**
- Link based solely on keyword overlap without semantic relationship
- Create a dedicated "connections" or "see also" section — if it's not in the prose, it's not a connection
- Link every page in the same category to each other

## Lint-based link discovery

Automated agent pass for existing wikis that lack inline links:
1. Build alias map: page slug → matching text patterns (full name, course code, key phrase)
2. Scan prose for mentions of other pages
3. Replace first occurrence with `[[slug|matched text]]`
4. Conservative: skip ambiguous matches
5. Flag pages with 0-1 outbound links for review

## Renderer support

- **Inline links**: render `[[slug|Display Text]]` as clickable links showing only display text
- **Backlinks panel**: auto-generated list of pages that link TO this page — this replaces any need for a "connections" section
- Optional: mini knowledge graph showing 1-hop connections from current page

title: Agent setup: add password save cues + make username apparent
Meta: document all agent instruction surfaces that need sync
Curator Agent: inline sidebar agent for wiki curation (Snow Crash / Ready Player One inspired)
description:
## Vision

An inline curator agent sidebar in the spirit of the Librarian from Snow Crash and the Curator from Ready Player One. An AI assistant that lives in the WikiHub UI sidebar and can take actions on your behalf — curate, cross-reference, promote content between wikis.

## Example Use Case

You're browsing someone's idea list. You want to promote an idea to the Featured Wikis page. The curator agent can:
1. Read the current page context (who you are, what page you're on)
2. Take actions with your permissions (read/write wikis you have access to)
3. Append content to public-edit pages, create pages in your own wikis, etc.

## Architecture Considerations

### Agent Backend
- **Claude Code under the hood** (like Noos project) — coding agent with file/API tools
- Should run in **print mode** for streaming responses to the sidebar
- Needs the current user's API key for WikiHub API calls
- Must be aware of: logged-in user, current page URL, current wiki context

### Security / Isolation
- Agent runs with the permissions of the current user (their API key)
- Must NOT get access to wrong directories on the server
- **MVP: Unix user permissions** may be sufficient — each agent session runs as a restricted user with only API access (no filesystem access beyond temp dirs)
- **Future: Docker containers** for full isolation if needed
- Agent should only interact via the WikiHub API, not touch git repos directly

### Parallel Agents
- Architecture should support multiple users running curator agents simultaneously
- Each agent session is stateless — gets user context at start, runs, returns result
- Server-side: queue or spawn model per request

### UI
- Sidebar panel on the right side of wiki pages
- Chat-like interface: user types a request, agent streams a response
- Agent can show proposed changes before applying them
- Minimizable/collapsible

### Chrome Extension (v2)
- Personal agent sidebar that works on any website
- If Chrome extension sidebar is open, WikiHub's built-in sidebar hides (avoid duplication)
- Extension would use the same WikiHub API key
- Relates to the Cortex project for personal agent infrastructure
- Could detect if user is logged into WikiHub and auto-configure

## MVP Scope (v1)
1. Right sidebar panel on wiki pages (behind feature flag or logged-in only)
2. Chat input → sends to Claude API with system prompt including page context
3. Agent can call WikiHub API endpoints (search, read_page, create_page, update_page, append_section)
4. Streams response back to sidebar
5. No filesystem access — API-only

## Open Questions
- Unix permissions vs Docker for agent isolation?
- Should the agent have its own API key or use the user's?
- Rate limiting per user?
- How to handle the Chrome extension ↔ built-in sidebar coordination?

title: Sidebar: distinguish folders from cross-wiki links in navigation
description:
## Problem

When navigating the sidebar, there's no visual distinction between:
1. **Folders** — subfolders within the current wiki (e.g. `ideas/` folder inside `jacobcole/jacobcole`)
2. **Wiki links** — links that take you to a different wiki entirely (e.g. from personal wiki to `jacobcole/ideas`)

Both use the same folder/page icons and indentation. Users can't tell if clicking will keep them in the current wiki or jump to a different one.

Currently the sidebar only shows items from the current wiki's file tree. Cross-wiki links only appear in page content as regular markdown links. The profile page has a separate "Wikis" section, but this pattern isn't used elsewhere.

## Proposed Solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Wiki icon in sidebar (minimal)
Add a third icon type — a small "wiki" icon (e.g. book/globe/grid) — distinct from the folder and page icons. When the sidebar lists sub-wikis (e.g. on a profile or index page), use this icon instead of folder.

### Option B: "Your wikis" sidebar section
Like the profile page already does — show a separate labeled section "WIKIS" below the file tree in the sidebar. This already works on profile.html (lines 176-185). Extend to reader.html and folder.html when viewing a user's personal wiki.

### Option C: Breadcrumb-aware context switching
When you click from one wiki into another, the breadcrumb should make it visually obvious you've changed context — e.g. the sidebar header changes from "JACOB'S IDEAS" to the new wiki's name, with a "← back to profile" link. (This already happens, but the transition is jarring with no warning.)

### Option D: Wiki cards in page content
When the index page links to other wikis, render those as visually distinct cards (title + description + page count) rather than plain text links. This makes it obvious in the content area that these are whole wikis, not pages.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option B** — it's the simplest and profile.html already has the pattern. Then add **Option A** (wiki icon) for polish. Option D is nice for landing pages but is a bigger change.

## Files involved
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` builds the sidebar
- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 181-209)
- `app/templates/folder.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 47-69)
- `app/templates/profile.html` — already has the "Wikis" section pattern (lines 176-185)

title: Profile vs personal wiki: clicking a person's name should go to /@username, not /@username/username
description:
On the Explore page, wiki cards for personal wikis (where slug == username) link to /@username/username (the wiki root), not /@username (the profile). This is confusing because:

1. The profile page IS the personal wiki rendered — they show the same content
2. But the profile has the user header, wiki sidebar with project wikis, and 'Wikis' section
3. The wiki root is just the wiki content without the profile chrome

For personal wikis, links should go to /@username (the profile) everywhere:
- Explore page wiki cards
- Search results
- Any place that shows a personal wiki as a card/link

For project wikis (slug != username), links should go to /@username/slug as normal.

The deeper question: should there even be a difference between /@username and /@username/username? Currently the profile page renders the personal wiki's index.md as its main content. Maybe /@username/username should just redirect to /@username. That would make the personal wiki's web identity always be the profile URL, while git clone still uses /@username/username.git.

title: Epic: Auth & account completeness (Google-Drive readiness)
description:
# WikiHub auth & account design — "Google Drive readiness"

Status: draft, sending to Codex for review.

## Current state (verified from code, 2026-04-21)

User model fields: `id, username, display_name, email (unique, nullable), password_hash (nullable), google_id (unique, nullable), llm_api_key_encrypted, created_at`. **No `email_verified_at`.**

Auth surfaces that exist:
- `/auth/signup` — username + password (+ optional unverified email)
- `/auth/login` — username/password OR `?api_key=`
- `/auth/magic/<token>` — single-use link, 15-min TTL, requires caller to already hold an API key or password to mint
- `/auth/google` + `/auth/google/callback` — Google OAuth
- `POST /api/v1/accounts` — admin/API account creation

Gaps:
1. No forgot-password / password-reset flow
2. No email verification (email field is unique + nullable + unverified)
3. No "change password" UI in /settings
4. No "link Google" / "unlink Google" in /settings
5. No delete-account flow
6. No account merge for duplicate sign-ups

## Latent security issue: Google auto-link by unverified email

Current `google_callback()`:
```python
user = User.query.filter_by(google_id=google_id).first()
if not user and email:
    user = User.query.filter_by(email=email).first()
    if user:
        user.google_id = google_id  # <-- links without verification
```

**Takeover path**: Alice signs up with password, claims email=`victim@gmail.com` (unverified — no confirmation ever sent). Later Victim hits "Sign in with Google", their real Google email is `victim@gmail.com`. Server finds Alice's User by email, binds `google_id=<Victim>` to Alice's account. Victim now logs in and lands in *Alice's* account. Alice retains her password + API key, so she sees whatever Victim creates after. This is exploitable as long as email goes unverified.

**Fix**: never auto-link by email unless the email on the matching account is verified. If match exists but isn't verified, force the Google user down the new-account path (or prompt them to explicitly claim the email via verification).

## Proposed design

### A. Email verification (foundation for everything else)

Schema:
- Add `email_verified_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NULL`
- Change email uniqueness: partial unique index `WHERE email_verified_at IS NOT NULL`. Keep email column otherwise. This lets two unrelated users both claim `foo@bar.com` as unverified (first to verify wins), instead of first-to-type.
- New table `EmailVerificationToken(id, user_id, new_email, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)`. We store `new_email` on the token, not on User, so the "current" email doesn't change until verification. TTL 24h.

Flows:
- Signup with email → create User with `email` set and `email_verified_at=null`. Mint verification token, email the link. User can log in immediately; UI shows "Verify your email" banner.
- Settings → "Change email" → mint verification token on new address; verification swaps email + sets verified timestamp.
- Google OAuth → Google-provided email is treated as verified iff Google's token `email_verified=true` (Google provides this). Set `email_verified_at=now()` when we create the account from Google.

### B. Password reset

Schema: `PasswordResetToken(id, user_id, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)`. TTL 30 min. Single use.

Flow:
- `POST /auth/forgot` with `{email}`. Always respond 204 (don't leak existence).
  - If a verified email matches a user with `password_hash` set, mint token + send email with reset link.
  - If matched user has no password (Google-only), email them saying "your account uses Google Sign-In; click here to sign in with Google" instead.
  - If no match, do nothing. Still respond 204.
- `GET /auth/reset/<token>` → form, new password + confirm.
- `POST /auth/reset/<token>` → verify token, update `password_hash`, mark token used, invalidate all of that user's sessions (force re-login), invalidate all other reset tokens for the user.

Rate limits: per-email (5/hour) and per-IP (20/hour). Both use the existing in-memory deque pattern from login/signup.

### C. /settings additions

- **Change password** — requires current password. Invalidates other sessions.
- **Change email** — starts verification flow (section A).
- **Linked accounts** — show Google linkage. "Link Google Account" (starts OAuth with `state=link&user_id=<me>`; callback branches on state). "Unlink Google" (only allowed if a password is set, else user would be locked out).
- **Delete account** — requires password or Google re-auth, soft-delete with 30-day grace (new `deleted_at` column, nightly hard-delete cron).

### D. Account merging

Two cases:
1. **Have Google-only account, want password**: not a merge — just "set password" in /settings.
2. **Two separate accounts exist (one password, one Google, different emails)**: explicit merge while signed in.

Proposed merge flow (manual, user-initiated):
- Signed in as account A. "Merge another account into this one" in /settings.
- Authenticate as account B (password form or Google sign-in with `state=merge_source&into=A`).
- Show confirmation: "Everything owned by @B will move to @A. @B will be deleted. This cannot be undone."
- On confirm: reassign `owner_id` on wikis, api_keys, stars, forks, audit_log rows; rename git repos (`repos/B/foo.git` → `repos/A/foo.git`, respecting slug collisions by appending `-b` suffix); delete B.

Risks to name explicitly in ticket:
- Slug collisions (A and B both have a wiki slug `notes`) — we have to rename one.
- Subdomain collisions (wikis have globally-unique subdomains) — likely already safe since B's subdomain is distinct, but verify.
- External references (published URLs like `https://B.wikihub.md` → break unless we 301 to `A.wikihub.md`).
- Git clone URLs in users' local repos stop working after merge.

Given that risk surface, propose we ship "set password on Google-only account" (case 1) now, and defer the cross-account merge (case 2) until someone actually asks for it. It's a lot of code for a rare need.

### E. Fixing the Google auto-link hole

In `google_callback`:
```python
user = User.query.filter_by(google_id=google_id).first()
if not user and email and email_verified_from_google:
    candidate = User.query.filter_by(email=email).first()
    if candidate and candidate.email_verified_at is not None:
        candidate.google_id = google_id
        user = candidate
    # else: fall through to "create new account" below
```

Only link if both sides verified the email. This should ship alongside the email-verification column so we don't retro-link existing unverified matches.

## Recommended shipping order

Each is a beads subtask under wikihub-ks5t.

1. Schema migration: add `email_verified_at`, swap to partial unique index. Backfill `email_verified_at=NULL` for everyone (no one is verified today).
2. Email-sending pipeline (pick provider — Postmark? Resend? AWS SES? — smallest footprint). Single module with `send_transactional(to, subject, body)`.
3. Email verification flow + banner in header when unverified. On signup, send verification. On Google sign-in, mark verified if Google says so.
4. Fix Google auto-link (section E). Requires #1 and #3.
5. Password reset flow (`/auth/forgot`, `/auth/reset/<token>`). Requires #2 and #3.
6. /settings: change password, change email, linked accounts.
7. Delete account (soft + cron).
8. (Maybe later) cross-account merge.

## Open questions

- Email provider: Postmark is cheapest-to-start and has good deliverability, but costs ~$15/mo. AWS SES is ~free but needs reputation warm-up + bounce handling. Resend is developer-friendly. Leaning Postmark for MVP. **Ask Jacob.**
- Should unverified-email signups get full access, or gate wiki creation on verification? Leaning full access with a banner — friction at signup loses users. Gate only "destructive" or "public" actions (publishing a wiki to subdomain?) if needed later.
- Session-invalidation on password change — we don't currently track sessions in DB; Flask-Login cookies are signed tokens. To "invalidate all sessions" we'd need to bump a `session_version` on the user and check it on every request. Propose adding that column now.
- Delete account: also delete git repos or keep them? Keep for grace period, then purge. Need to document in privacy policy.

title: NOTABLE.md note is wrong: API accounts CAN log in via web UI

title: Google OAuth should preserve next destination and invite context
description:
Workflow audit finding from 2026-04-22.

Current auth templates link straight to `/auth/google`, and `google_callback()` redirects to `/`. That drops `next`, invite email, and invite token context, so Google sign-in/sign-up cannot continue where the user was going.

Likely fix:
- stash validated `next`, `email`, and invite token in session / OAuth state before redirect
- restore them in the callback
- apply pending invites on successful Google auth
- add e2e coverage for invite + OAuth redirect preservation if feasible

Evidence:
- app/templates/auth/login.html
- app/templates/auth/signup.html
- app/routes/auth.py google_login/google_callback
- workflow audit report /tmp/wikihub-workflow-audit.md

title: Explore Editorial Picks keep getting clobbered — need data-driven curation
description:
## Problem (recurring)

The Explore page's Editorial Picks section keeps losing its curated wikis. This has happened multiple times. The picks are currently hardcoded in main.py as a list of (owner, slug) tuples, which means any refactor to the explore route can accidentally drop them.

## Current Fix

Hardcoded _EDITORIAL_PICKS list in app/routes/main.py with curated wikis:
- wikihub/wikihub, jacobcole/ideas, jacobcole/quotes, jacobcole/rsi-wiki, jacobcole/admitsphere, jacobcole/llm-wiki-ecosystem, harrisonqian/tools-stack

## Better Long-term Solution

Move editorial picks to a data source (not code):
1. A DB table `editorial_picks` with wiki_id + sort_order
2. Or a config file / admin endpoint to manage picks
3. Or derive from the Featured Wikis page in wikihub/wikihub wiki

This way code refactors can't accidentally drop curated content.

## How to Verify

Visit /explore and check that the "Editorial Picks" section shows multiple wiki cards, not just WikiHub.

Scope should cover desktop and mobile behavior, permission gating, and how this interacts with collapsed folders and future custom ordering work.

title: No CSRF protection on login/signup forms
description: Login, signup, and test-login forms have no CSRF tokens. Combined with missing SameSite cookie attribute, this enables cross-site request forgery attacks. Fix: add Flask-WTF or manual CSRF tokens to all state-changing forms.

title: Per-person sharing: generalized share/unshare/list-grants API
External wiki references/aliases in sidebar — cross-wiki navigation
description:
Allow wikis to reference or alias other wikis (even from different users) in their sidebar navigation. Use case: the @wikihub community wiki could have a 'Featured Wikis' folder in its sidebar that lists @jacobcole/rsi-wiki, @jacobcole/admitsphere, etc. as if they were local pages.

This is conceptually like symlinks or bookmarks in a filesystem — you don't own the content, but you can navigate to it from your wiki's sidebar.

Possible implementations:
- A .wikihub/references file that lists external wiki paths to include in sidebar
- A special frontmatter field like 'sidebar_refs' on index.md
- A folder-like virtual entry that links to another wiki

This would make the community index wiki much more useful as a navigational hub — not just a page with links, but actual sidebar entries that feel like part of the wiki.

Inspired by how Quartz and other static site generators handle cross-references.

title: Admin page HTML served without auth at /api/v1/admin/claude-auth
description: The admin Claude auth page template is served to any visitor at /api/v1/admin/claude-auth (HTTP 200, 8.7KB HTML). The API endpoints behind it correctly check admin token, but serving the admin UI to unauthenticated users is minor info disclosure. The page render should also require the admin token.

title: GET-based onboarding for vanilla LLM agents (web_fetch with only GET)
description:
Stretch goal noted 2026-04-22: enable a vanilla LLM (Claude.ai, ChatGPT, Gemini built-in tools) with **GET-only** web_fetch to onboard to wikihub by following instructions in AGENTS.md — no Custom GPT Action, no code exec, just a URL to hit.

## Why

Today, to onboard an agent requires `POST /api/v1/accounts`. Vanilla LLM web-fetch tools are GET-only. That's a sharp cliff in who can onboard without human copy-paste.

## Possible design

- `GET /onboard?username=<handle>` — creates an account, returns JSON with API key
- Rate-limit aggressively: 1/IP/hour; reject bots via basic heuristics
- Must still produce the same `client_config` JSON as POST so the agent can paste it into its credentials file
- Optionally require a confirmation URL click — but that defeats the whole 'vanilla LLM' use case

## Risks

- GET-with-side-effects violates HTTP semantics (caching, prefetch, URL previews can trigger creation)
- Easier spam surface — abuse bots love GET
- Search engines + screenshots could leak API keys if the response ever renders in HTML (never render it — JSON only)

## Alternatives

- Keep as-is, document copy-paste curl workflow in AGENTS.md prominently (today's answer)
- Ship a 'signup magic URL' — user clicks, browser-only, returns JSON. Still not vanilla-LLM-friendly.
- Ship an MCP/Agent Skill plugin that third-party LLMs can install (what Cortex does) — highest lift

## Decision

Deferred. Needs product judgment call before implementation. File captures the aspiration; do not implement until explicit green-light from Jacob.

See also: `wikihub-<one-command-spec>` for the invariant this would extend.

title: Landing page zip upload lost after signup redirect
description: User uploads a zip on the landing page, gets told it'll create after signin. They click signin, get redirected to signin (not signup), sign up instead, and after account creation the zip upload is gone — the wiki is never created. The upload state needs to survive the auth redirect flow.

title: Cmd+K search: context-aware scoping to current wiki/author
rate limiting for API write endpoints
description: in-memory sliding window (10 writes/min/user) on all page CRUD endpoints. prevents agent floods from monopolizing gunicorn workers via regenerate_public_mirror.

title: Decide collaborator git-write architecture (pick between options 1-5)
description:
Decide collab write architecture: fork+PR (3), per-collaborator view repos (2), staging-refs Gerrit-style (5), or punt (4). Codex second-opinion picked option 3 with option 5 as a polished variant; see comments on wikihub-3se7.

Key constraints any design must satisfy:
- `.wikihub/acl` is content — a collaborator who can write it can escalate.
- Frontmatter `visibility:` is content — same escalation risk.
- Rename/move and deletion semantics need explicit rules (a collab shouldn't delete or rename a path they can't read).
- Git's trust model is repo-level. Path-level read filtering inside a single repo is not something Git natively supports and is where leaks happen.

Out of scope for this ticket: the stopgap UX (tracked separately). This ticket is "decide and design" — pick the architecture, write it down, then split into implementation tickets. No coding until a direction is chosen.

title: Per-person sharing: 'Shared with me' dashboard + API
One-click navigation back to own profile from any page
description: Currently getting back to your own profile requires opening the account dropdown menu then clicking 'View profile' — two clicks. Should be one click from anywhere. Options: (1) clicking the avatar/initial in the nav goes directly to profile, (2) add a persistent 'Home' link in the nav, (3) the [[wikihub]] logo could go to your profile when logged in instead of the landing page. Need to decide which pattern fits best.

title: Restructure Jeremy Nixon wiki to Karpathy LLM Wiki format with daily logs
Edit button missing for unauthenticated users on public-edit pages
description:

title: Add share button to page view (reader.html) for wiki owners
Email verification flow: signup sends verify link, banner until verified, Google emails auto-verified
description: Add `EmailVerificationToken(id, user_id, new_email, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)` with 24h TTL, single-use. Signup with email: create user with email set, email_verified_at=NULL, mint token, send link. /auth/verify/<token>: swap email on User, set email_verified_at=now(). Settings flow: 'Change email' mints a token on the new address; only swaps on click. Google OAuth: when Google's id_token has email_verified=true, set email_verified_at=now() at account creation. Add a dismissible 'verify your email' banner in base template when current user has an email but email_verified_at is null. Don't gate any actions on verification yet.

title: private wikis leak page existence and index content to unauthenticated users
description:
Two related access control issues with private wikis:

1. **Index page visible on private wikis** — if a wiki is fully private, visiting the wiki index still renders the page. It should deny access entirely (redirect to login or show a 'no access' message).

2. **Direct links to private pages show empty page shell** — if you have a direct link to a private page (e.g. from a search engine or shared URL), the route renders the page template with no content instead of showing a clear 'no access' or 'this page is private' message. This leaks that the page exists and looks like a bug to the user.

Expected behavior:
- Private wiki index: 403/404 or redirect, not a rendered page
- Direct link to private page: clear 'no access' message, not an empty page shell

title: EPIC: Programmatic test coverage parity — agent can verify before human tests
description:
Goal: **an agent (me, codex, any future session) can verify every user-facing flow programmatically before Jacob has to click through anything.** Today we have 45 e2e tests, but real bugs keep slipping past because whole flows are untested.

## Why now

Recent bugs that a test would have caught:
- **wikihub-35gh** — prod git push fails because pre-receive hook can't import Flask. No git push e2e test exists.
- **/people 404 on user subdomains** — subdomain test only covered apex paths.
- **Reader share modal** — audit misflagged the invited-response handling because no JS-level test exists to prove the modal works.

These are all programmatic flows. If we had coverage, I could catch them before shipping and before Jacob has to manually test.

## Coverage gaps (what to add, in priority order)

### Critical (P0-class bugs are slipping through here)
1. **Git clone + push e2e** — spin a test client, clone a wiki over Smart HTTP, make a commit, push, verify the hook ran, verify DB-sync landed. Must run against a real pre-receive hook installation.
2. **Subdomain matrix** — for every global route (/people, /shared, /explore, /settings, /auth/*, /.well-known/*), test both apex AND user-subdomain host. Parameterize over routes; a new main_bp route should automatically get tested on subdomains.
3. **Full browser-level smoke** via Playwright or chrome-devtools headless — every click path a real user takes (signup → confirmation email → click link → land on wiki). Tests need to cover the JS, not just the HTML.

### High
4. **Google OAuth round-trip** — mock the Google OIDC endpoints, simulate the callback, verify user creation, email verification state, and pending-invite materialization.
5. **MCP server tool invocations** — stand up the MCP server locally, invoke each of the 19 tools, assert responses. Source: mcp-server/ directory.
6. **Editor save flow from the UI** — POST to the editor endpoint with form data, verify Page row updates, verify git commit landed.
7. **File upload (binary)** — multipart POST of PNG/PDF/etc, verify served correctly with right content-type, verify ACL.

### Medium
8. **Wiki deletion cascade** — delete a wiki with stars/forks/wikilinks, verify referential integrity.
9. **Fork operations** — fork a wiki, push to fork, verify source is untouched.
10. **Reader page rendering** — given markdown with wikilinks/KaTeX/footnotes, verify the rendered HTML has the expected structure.
11. **Real email delivery** — live Cloudflare Email Service integration test (gated by env var so CI doesn't spam).

## The maintenance rule (to prevent regressing to this state)

**Add to AGENTS.md: "every bug fix MUST include a regression test that would have failed before the fix."** Specifically:

- If a bug is filed (`bd type=bug`), the fix PR must add or modify a test that demonstrates the bug and now passes.
- If the bug is a "programmatically untestable" class (browser behavior, real email, real DNS), the fix must either add the testing infrastructure OR file a dependency ticket for that infra before closing the bug.
- Agents reviewing their own work should explicitly ask: "would my change pass if the exact bug were reintroduced?" If not, the test is insufficient.

Ticket hygiene:
- `bd label add <closed-bug-id> needs-regression-test` on any closed bug that didn't get a test
- Monthly sweep: go through `bd list --label needs-regression-test` and add tests or downgrade to "won't fix"

## Tooling asks

- Playwright or chrome-devtools-mcp integrated into the test runner (currently only used ad-hoc)
- A CI that runs the full suite on every push (currently only local + manual)
- A pre-deploy hook that runs at least the auth + git flows before `systemctl restart wikihub`

## Definition of done for this epic

An agent can run `python3 tests/test_e2e.py` and be confident that if it passes, **no user-facing flow is broken**. Today: false. Goal: true. Close this epic only when a fresh agent could take any open ticket and not need human test assistance.
