title: Design community index wiki (@wikihub as curated meta-index)
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub wiki should serve as a curated community index — an 'awesome list of awesome lists.' User stories:

1. A user creates a great 'Best Body Masters List' wiki. They want it featured.
2. The community wiki has a page like 'Best Fitness Wikis' that links to it with a blurb.
3. A visitor lands on @wikihub and can browse curated categories pointing to the best user wikis.

This is different from /explore (algorithmic, by stars/activity). The community wiki is editorial — someone decides what's featured.

Key questions to resolve:
- Is @wikihub/wikihub the right place for this, or should there be a separate @wikihub/featured wiki?
- How do categories work? Pages in the wiki like 'Best AI Tools Wikis', 'Best Reading Lists'?
- Should featured wikis get a badge/indicator on their own pages?
- Who can edit the community index? Just admins, or suggested-edit PRs from users?
- How does this interplay with the Explore page? Does Explore pull from the community wiki's editorial picks?

For v1: probably just manually curated pages in @wikihub/wikihub that link to user wikis with [[cross-wiki wikilinks]] or plain URLs. The index IS the wiki — no special machinery needed. Editorial curation is just writing markdown.

Reference: ListHub had similar brainstorming about community lists as indexes of other lists.

title: Read-only collaboration: suggest edits or request write access
description: Design a path for pages where the viewer only has public-view or read access. Options to evaluate: 1) allow non-destructive suggested edits on read-only pages, 2) offer an explicit request-write-access flow, or 3) both with clear permission semantics. Scope should cover UI entrypoints on the reader page, notification/review path for owners, and how it relates to the broader suggestion/comment system.

title: Anonymous posting (claimable or non-claimable) across CLI, API, UI
description:
Support posting content anonymously, with two modes:

1. **Claimable anonymous** (DEFAULT) — post has no author attribution, but any authenticated user can claim authorship later (becomes their post going forward).
2. **Non-claimable anonymous** — post has no author attribution and can never be attached to an account. Permanently anonymous.

**Default when a user selects 'anonymous': claimable=true** (updated 2026-04-21 per user).

Must work consistently across all three surfaces:
- **API**: new fields on page create/update (`anonymous: true`, `claimable: true|false`); new `POST /api/v1/.../claim` endpoint for claim flow
- **CLI**: `--anonymous` and `--claimable/--no-claimable` flags on page-creation commands; `wikihub claim <page>` command
- **UI**: checkbox/toggle in the page editor; 'Claim this page' button visible on claimable anonymous posts when logged in

Open design questions (resolve before implementing):
- ~~Default: is anonymous posting claimable or non-claimable by default?~~ RESOLVED: claimable=true
- Abuse: anyone can claim (user's explicit design). First-come-first-served. No original-author-token protection. Spam/impersonation risk accepted.
- Storage: how is anonymous authorship represented in Postgres (null user_id? synthetic 'anonymous' user?) and in git (what goes in the commit author field — proposed: 'Anonymous <anonymous@wikihub.md>')
- ACLs: anonymous posts inherit wiki's default visibility. Private wikis can have anonymous posts.
- Audit log: existing audit_log already tracks IP/session — no new tracking needed.

title: Profile says 1 wiki but lists none
description: After signup, the profile page shows '1 wiki' (the auto-provisioned personal wiki) but the wiki list below is empty — contradictory. Either show the personal wiki in the list or say 0 wikis.

title: Warn when multiple editors have a page open at once
description:
Precursor to real-time collaboration. Before two people can edit the same page at the same time, we need a live warning banner when another editor is concurrently viewing/editing the same page.

Scope:
- Detect concurrent editors on /edit routes (heartbeat, presence pings, or similar)
- Show a live warning in the editor UI: 'X is also editing this page'
- Don't block save yet — just surface the conflict risk so users can coordinate out-of-band
- Decide on a presence mechanism: polling, SSE, WebSocket, or Redis pub/sub

Out of scope (for this ticket):
- Actual real-time collaborative editing (CRDTs, Yjs, OT)
- Conflict resolution on save (already handled via content_hash/If-Match)
- Cursors or live character sync

This is a 'before we add X, we should do Y' ticket — blocker/precursor for any real-time collab work.

title: Build jacob-tejas-strategy llmwiki from Gemini meeting notes
description:
Build a Karpathy-style llmwiki from 4 Jacob/Tejas Strategy meetings (Mar 26–Apr 15, 2026) already pulled from Gemini-notes Gmail + Google Drive transcripts. Raw source files on disk at `scratch/jacob-tejas-strategy/raw/` (4 summaries + 4 full transcripts).

## Two proposed schemas

### Schema A — meetings-first (initial proposal)

```
raw/
  summaries/YYYY-MM-DD.md   ← Gemini email summaries (verbatim)
  transcripts/YYYY-MM-DD.md ← full Google Doc transcripts (verbatim)
topics/
  wikihub-vs-graph.md
  linear-adoption.md
  openclaw-roadmap.md
  payroll-issues.md
  action-items.md
index.md
```

Premise: one page per meeting (summary + transcript) as the raw layer; synthesized topic pages cross-link back with wikilinks; action-items aggregated.

### Schema B — threads-first (Codex revision, preferred)

```
index.md
wiki/
  now.md
  monthly/2026-04.md
  threads/
    file-vs-graph.md
    wikihub-to-nuos-transition.md
    velocity-vs-quality.md
    prioritization-and-founder-bandwidth.md
    linear-adoption-and-planning.md
    openclaw-onboarding-and-group-chat.md
    financial-pressure-and-payroll.md
log/
  meetings/YYYY-MM-DD.md   ← *delta* pages (cleaned, what changed this week)
  timeline.md
schema/
  decisions.md
  action-items.md
  entities.md
sources/
  summaries/   ← untouched imports
  transcripts/
```

Key refinements vs A:
- Durable unit is the **recurring thread**, not the meeting or the product.
- `sources/` (untouched artifacts) is separate from `log/` (cleaned chronological deltas).
- Meeting pages record only the delta on each thread. Full arguments live on canonical thread pages.
- Aggressive synthesis, conservative quoting. Verbatim only for load-bearing phrases ("wrong abstraction", "virality coefficient", "saying yes to everything").
- Load-bearing wikilinks only: meeting→threads, thread→products/people/decisions. Cap at 3–6 per meeting page.

## First pages to create (Schema B)

1. `index.md` — landing, active threads, latest monthly, latest decisions
2. `wiki/monthly/2026-04.md` — 2-minute founder skim
3. `wiki/threads/file-vs-graph.md` — core architectural tension
4. `wiki/threads/wikihub-to-nuos-transition.md` — 24+36hr plan, whether it holds
5. `wiki/threads/velocity-vs-quality.md` — Apr 8 tension
6. `wiki/threads/prioritization-and-founder-bandwidth.md` — the "saying yes to everything" pattern
7. `wiki/threads/linear-adoption-and-planning.md` — process change
8. `wiki/threads/openclaw-onboarding-and-group-chat.md` — Telegram flows
9. `wiki/threads/financial-pressure-and-payroll.md` — Mar 26 payroll discussion
10. `schema/decisions.md` + `schema/action-items.md`

## Target

Publish as `wikihub.md/@jacobcole/jacob-tejas-strategy` once scaffolded locally.

## Open question

Whether to have Codex generate first-pass scaffolds with starter frontmatter + section templates for the 8 core pages, or hand-build.

title: Subdomain assignment strategy — auto-claim vs opt-in, collision policy, opt-out
description:
Current (shipped in wikihub-3fjb): subdomains are OPT-IN per wiki. Owner claims at /@user/slug/settings. jacobcole has 15 wikis, only rsi-wiki claimed a subdomain.

## Options on the table

### 1. Keep opt-in (current)
- Wikis stay at <user>.wikihub.md/<slug> until owner claims a subdomain.
- Pros: no automatic collisions, no migration needed, user controls branding.
- Cons: most wikis never get the nicer URL; wiki subdomain feature is underused.

### 2. Auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served
- On POST /api/v1/wikis, try to claim the slug as a subdomain. If collision, fall back to empty (still accessible under user subdomain path).
- Pros: most wikis auto-get the nice URL. Aligns with jacob's maximalist preference.
- Cons: early users hog common slugs (ideas, notes, recipes, etc.). Collision = silent fallback unless we surface it.

### 3. Auto-claim with <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (sub-subdomains)
- Every wiki auto-gets <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (no global collisions).
- Pros: no collisions ever, every wiki gets a nice branded URL.
- Cons: nginx + Cloudflare wildcard-of-wildcard complexity. Cloudflare doesn't support *.*.domain — requires SaaS addon or per-user DNS records. Long URLs.

### 4. Hybrid: auto-claim with namespace fallback
- Try <slug>.wikihub.md first. If taken, auto-assign <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md.
- Pros: common slugs still work for the first claimer, everyone else gets namespaced.
- Cons: mixed URL schemes across the product. Inconsistency.

### 5. Bring-your-own-domain (BYOD)
- Let users CNAME their own domain (e.g., notes.example.com → wikihub origin).
- Pros: max flexibility, no wikihub.md URL at all.
- Cons: per-domain SSL provisioning (Caddy on-demand, or Cloudflare for SaaS), DNS ops.

## User control primitives needed regardless
- [ ] Opt-out of subdomain (nullable already — setting to null works)
- [ ] Rename / change subdomain post-creation (API already supports — works)
- [ ] Reserved-word enforcement (already in subdomains.py RESERVED_SUBDOMAINS)
- [ ] Username-collision check (already checks via username_conflicts)

## Personal recommendation (for review)
Start with #2 (auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served) for MVP. Surface collisions in the settings UI: "subdomain <X> is taken, claim something else or keep path URL". Collect feedback, layer in #3 or #5 only if needed.

## Decision owner
jacobcole — opinion TBD, initial lean is #2.

## Related
- Closed: wikihub-3fjb (initial opt-in implementation shipped 2026-04-20)
- Open: real-time collab (wikihub-w0s4) which targets staging.wikihub.md

Update project references from wikihub.globalbr.ai → wikihub.md
description:
`wikihub.md` is the canonical public URL. `wikihub.globalbr.ai` still resolves (and should keep resolving as a redirect), but it shouldn't be what the project hands out to users, documents, or defaults to in config.

Inventory (from `grep -r "wikihub\.globalbr\.ai"` on 2026-04-20):

## Must update (user-facing / developer-facing)
- `app/credentials_hint.py:14` — default `server` URL embedded in the `client_config` JSON returned to new users at signup. Every new user's `~/.wikihub/credentials.json` will currently point at the old domain.
- `electron/main.js:23` — Electron app's fallback production base URL.
- `electron/main.js:268` — "View docs" menu item link target.
- `AGENTS.md:60` — "Live URL" field (main README entry).
- `AGENTS.md:144` — post-deploy smoke-test URL.
- `AGENTS.md:156` — post-deploy curl command for the 200-not-502 check.
- `docs/deploy.md:47` — smoke-test curl.
- `docs/deploy.md:63` — agent-browser target.
- `docs/deploy.md:64` — search API test URL.

## Low-priority but clean
- `mcp_server.py:9` — usage example in docstring.
- `scripts/scrape_gdocs.py:20` — `WIKIHUB_BASE` constant.

## Leave alone
- `app/subdomains.py:15` — comment explains that `wikihub.globalbr.ai` is a legacy host that the subdomain router explicitly redirects. The context is load-bearing. Acceptable change: reword to make it unambiguously historical (e.g., "the legacy `wikihub.globalbr.ai` deploy host still redirects here").
- `scripts/migrate_jeremynixon.py:15` — one-time migration script, already run.
- `.beads/issues.jsonl` — historical closed tickets; leaving the old URL is part of the historical record.
- `.playwright-mcp/console-*.log` — old test logs.

## Acceptance
1. `grep -r "wikihub\.globalbr\.ai"` returns only the four acceptable locations (`subdomains.py` comment, migrate script, beads history, playwright log).
2. A newly created user's `client_config` returns `"server": "https://wikihub.md"`.
3. `AGENTS.md` and `docs/deploy.md` smoke-test commands hit `wikihub.md` and still pass (both domains resolve).
4. The Electron app's "View docs" menu opens `https://wikihub.md/agents`.

## Out of scope
- Deprecating `wikihub.globalbr.ai` entirely. The redirect should continue working indefinitely — old links in the wild shouldn't 404.
- Cloudflare / nginx config changes (the redirect is already in place).

## Related context
- Canonical URL decision documented in memory: `~/.claude/projects/-Users-jacobcole-code-wikihub/memory/reference_wikihub_canonical_url.md`
- Recent cross-wiki linkage fixed on 2026-04-20 in ideaflow-vision + vision-convo content (not code) as part of `wikihub-4i3b`.

title: Epic: Obsidian feature parity audit — autocomplete, page creation, conventions
Git remote add UI for owners + push access model for collaborators
description:
## Part 1: Owner "Add Remote" UI (do now)

Add a second command in the clone dropdown, visible only to the wiki owner:

```
CLONE
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──┐
│ git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@u/wiki.git  │⎘│
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──┘

PUSH AN EXISTING REPO (owner only)
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──┐
│ git remote add wikihub https://.../@u/wiki.git     │⎘│
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──┘
```

Plus a subtle info note: "Use your API key as the password when pushing."

Also add documentation on the WikiHub docs wiki (Getting Started or Agent Integration page) explaining git push/pull workflow.

## Part 2: Pathway to GitHub-style collaboration (think about later)

Currently only the wiki owner can push. To get to GitHub-level collaboration:

### What GitHub has that we don't (yet)
1. **Collaborator invites** — owner grants write access to specific users
2. **Fork + PR workflow** — fork a wiki, make changes, submit a pull request
3. **Branch-based PRs** — work on a branch, review diff, merge
4. **Per-branch permissions** — protect main, allow feature branches
5. **Write access via deploy keys** — CI/CD agents can push

### Possible WikiHub pathway
- **Phase 1 (now):** Owner-only push. Clone is public read-only. Fork creates your own copy.
- **Phase 2:** Collaborator model — owner can grant write access to other WikiHub users (stored in .wikihub/acl or a new collaborators table). Collaborators can push to the authoritative repo.
- **Phase 3:** PR-like workflow — forked wikis can propose changes back to the upstream wiki. Owner reviews a diff and merges. Could be lightweight (just a "propose merge" button that shows the diff) rather than full GitHub PRs.
- **Phase 4:** Branch support — multiple branches per wiki, branch-level permissions.

### Key architectural questions
- ACL file already supports per-user permissions — could extend to grant push access
- post-receive hook already syncs git→DB — collaborator pushes would just work
- The two-repo model (auth + public mirror) is already set up for this
- Do we need branches? Or is the fork-and-merge model enough for wikis?

### What's unique to WikiHub
Unlike GitHub, wiki collaboration is often about *content* not *code*. A simpler model might work:
- "Suggest edit" button that creates a pending change (no git knowledge needed)
- Owner approves/rejects inline, like Wikipedia's pending changes
- This would be more accessible than fork+PR for non-technical wiki contributors

## Part 3: Public/Private fork concerns (IMPORTANT)

The two-repo architecture creates real problems for the fork+PR model:

### The core issue
When you fork someone's wiki, you get a copy of the **public mirror** — the derived repo with private pages stripped out. This means:

1. **Information loss on fork**: Private pages, private bands, and .wikihub/acl are absent from the fork. The fork is an incomplete snapshot.

2. **Merge-back conflicts**: If a forked wiki proposes changes back to the upstream, the upstream has files the fork never had. Merging could be messy — the fork's commit history doesn't share a common ancestor with the authoritative repo's full tree.

3. **Accidental exposure risk**: If someone forks a wiki that later makes pages private, the fork still has the old public version of those pages. Forks are snapshots — they don't retroactively remove content. (Same as GitHub, but worth calling out since WikiHub has page-level visibility.)

4. **Collaborator push vs fork-merge**: If a collaborator pushes directly to the authoritative repo, they could theoretically see private pages in the git history. The git backend would need to either:
   - Route collaborators to the authoritative repo (they see everything — simple but scary)
   - Route collaborators to a branch on the authoritative repo (more isolated)
   - Keep collaborators on the public mirror with a merge-request queue (safest, most complex)

5. **ACL sync on merge**: If upstream merges changes from a fork, the ACL file in the fork won't match upstream's ACL. The merge needs to preserve upstream's ACL, not the fork's.

### Recommendations to think about
- Fork should record `forked_from` metadata so we can trace lineage
- "Propose changes" should be a diff-based review, not a git merge — avoids the two-repo mismatch
- Collaborator access should probably be "can push to authoritative repo" (simple) with a trust model (you only invite people you trust with your private pages)
- Consider: should forks of wikis with private pages show a warning? "This fork contains only public pages from the original"

title: Decision: Cloudflare Email Service vs Resend (resolved)
description:
Decision log, 2026-04-21:

**Decision:** Use Cloudflare Email Service for wikihub (not Resend).

**Reasoning:**
- $5/mo Workers Paid already committed (unlocks containers, D1 unlimited, Hyperdrive, etc.)
- Email is $0/mo for first 3k/month, then $0.35/1k flat — cheaper than Resend at every scale
- Domain onboarding auto-creates SPF/DKIM/DMARC in the Cloudflare zone (wikihub.md is already there)
- Single vendor / single dashboard / single billing

**Trade-offs accepted:**
- Public beta — less battle-tested than Resend for deliverability
- From Flask (Lightsail), we use REST API instead of Workers binding — slightly less elegant than native binding but fully supported

**Integration path:**
- REST endpoint: `POST https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts/{account_id}/email-service/send`
- Auth: Bearer token with Email Sending scope
- Send domain: wikihub.md (to be onboarded in CF dashboard)
- Sender addresses: noreply@wikihub.md, invites@wikihub.md

**Considered alternatives:**
- Resend: Cortex pattern, known good, but $20/mo floor for custom domain (free tier 1-domain limit blocked wikihub.md). Would add a second vendor.
- MailChannels: free tier for CF Workers ended Aug 2024 — not viable.
- AWS SES: wikihub is on Lightsail but switching would require separate identity verification + warm-up. No infrastructure lock-in win.

See wikihub-exj1 for implementation.

title: Email notifications on share (Cloudflare Email Service)
description:
Send an email when a wiki is shared with someone (existing user OR pending invite). Required for Jacob's team-onboarding flow.

**Decision (2026-04-21): Provider = Resend.** In progress by parallel agent. Do not touch email-sending infra in other tickets until this lands.

**Infra first:**
- Decide email provider: SES (already on AWS Lightsail), Postmark, Resend, or SMTP relay → **RESEND**
- Add env vars: EMAIL_PROVIDER, EMAIL_FROM, provider-specific keys (stored in /opt/wikihub-app/.env)
- Basic email-sending helper (sync first; queue later)
- Templated HTML+plaintext versions

**Email types:**
1. 'You were invited to a wiki' — for existing users (contains deep link)
2. 'You've been invited to create an account on wikihub.md' — for pending invites (contains signup link with email prefilled)
3. Optional: 'Your pending invite was applied' — when a new user signs up and inherits grants

**Respect:**
- Add an unsubscribe/prefs page (required in most jurisdictions)
- Rate-limit per recipient to prevent spam
- Don't email the sharer themselves

WikiHub can because wiki content is structured and searchable. Vision: WikiHub influencers - people known for curating great knowledge in specific domains.

title: Homepage: Get Started vs Sign In are different flows — audit duplication
Invite-link token verification (close the email-squatting hole)
description:
Security gap found in code review of wikihub-skp7/exj1 (2026-04-22).

## The hole

Today, signup auto-verifies + materializes pending invites for any account created with an email that has a `PendingInvite` row — purely based on DB existence check, with no proof that the signup-er actually received the invitation email.

**Attack:**
1. Attacker signs up with `victim@corp.com` (unverified) before any invite is sent
2. Owner bulk-shares a wiki to `victim@corp.com` → PendingInvite row
3. Attacker logs in, email matches pending invite → auto-verified → invite materializes
4. Attacker now has access intended for victim
5. Victim tries to sign up → 409 'email already registered', locked out

Bounded but real privilege escalation for private wikis.

Secondary issue: `_apply_pending_invites_on_login` reads `?email=` from request.args on POST login too, not just GET — slightly loosens the intended 'invite-link click = proof' semantics.

## Fix

1. **Token in invite link.** When creating a PendingInvite, generate a short random token (`secrets.token_urlsafe(24)`). Store on the PendingInvite row. Include in the email link as `?email=X&it=<token>`.
2. **Verify on signup/login.** Replace the current 'pending invite exists = auto-verify' rule with 'pending invite exists AND token matches'. Valid tokens come only from links we actually sent.
3. **Expiry.** Tokens valid for 30 days; expired → user must use normal email verification flow (wikihub-ks5t).
4. **Migration.** Add `token VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL` column to `pending_invites`. Backfill existing rows with a random token, re-send invite emails with new links (or accept that existing rows need a flow to regenerate).

## Why P0

Gap was introduced by the wikihub-skp7 ship today (2026-04-22). Jacob is about to send invites to his 4 IdeaFlow teammates — for those specific users the attack is impossible (their emails were only shared in this chat minutes ago). But for any public launch this needs to close.

title: Anonymous upload: drop files without signup → ephemeral account + claim flow
description:
Today dropping files as an anonymous user stashes them in IndexedDB and forces signup at /auth/login?next=/new, then replays on the new-wiki form. That's friction: the user came to try the product, not fill a form.

Proposed flow:
1. Anonymous user drops .md/folder/.zip on the hero drop zone.
2. Backend creates an ephemeral account ('anon-<8hex>', no password) and an API key.
3. Creates a wiki under that account (default public or unlisted — TBD).
4. Processes the uploaded files using the existing _process_uploads path.
5. Responds JSON with {wiki_url, username, api_key, claim_hint}.
6. Frontend shows a prominent one-time banner: 'Your wiki is live at X. Save your API key wh_... to keep editing it, or claim this account with an email.' Then navigates to the wiki.

Open questions:
- Default visibility for anon wikis — public or unlisted?
- Rate limiting per IP (see wikihub-ub2.1)
- 'Claim this account by email' is a follow-up, out of scope for v1.

Minimal v1: new endpoint POST /new-anonymous (CSRF-exempt), landing JS uses it when not logged in, result banner shown in-page before nav.

title: Hover preview cards leak when user moves to another link before fetch completes
description: In app/templates/base.html hover preview JS, openPreview() pushes a loading card onto stack then fetches. If user hovers a new link before fetch resolves, closeFromDepth() pops the loading card, but the fetch callback still appends a real card to document.body without re-adding it to stack. The orphan card has no owner and cannot be closed. Fix: in the fetch .then callback, after findIndex, return early if idx < 0 (stale response).

title: Per-wiki llms.txt endpoint

title: Create projects index page from homepage
Collaborator git push access with per-path ACL enforcement
description:
Today every wiki has a clone URL (/@user/slug.git) and two repos: authoritative (owner rw) + -public.git (non-owner ro mirror). ACL grants in .wikihub/acl give collaborators WEB edit access but git push is owner-only — receive_pack enforces user.username == owner.username.

What's needed:
- Non-owner collaborators with an :edit grant on some path (whole wiki, folder, or page) should be able to `git push` changes scoped to what their grants allow.
- Writes to paths they don't have :edit on must be rejected, ideally atomically (entire push fails rather than partial apply).

Design paths worth evaluating (get Codex second opinion before picking):

1. **Pre-receive hook ACL enforcement** — collaborator pushes to the authoritative repo; a pre-receive hook walks the pack, diffs changed paths, checks each against ACL grants, rejects the whole push if any path isn't allowed. Pros: one repo. Cons: fetch leaks private history; pack parsing is fiddly; risk of false-allow bugs.

2. **Per-collaborator view repos** — build <slug>-shared-<username>.git per collaborator containing only their visible + editable paths. They push there; server merges back into authoritative. Pros: clean visibility isolation. Cons: N repos per wiki, merge/conflict complexity, sync lag.

3. **Fork + PR flow** — collaborator forks (already supported), pushes to their fork, submits a PR. Simpler but not 'real git push access on the wiki'. Might be the right answer for contributor UX.

4. **Punt on git push for collabs; keep web-only edit for grants** — simplest; git push stays an owner-only power user feature. Document the tradeoff.

Mixed public/private complicates mirrors: a collaborator granted edit on notes/*.md needs a view where notes/ is present (including private notes shared with them) but private/secrets.md is absent. None of the current mirrors do that — public mirror is visibility-gated only, not user-gated.

Dependencies: this probably needs wikihub-ec48 (concurrent-edit warning) before real-time collab, but git-push collab is async so it could land independently.

Related: existing sharing UI (app/routes/api_wikis.py share endpoints) and ACL parser (app/acl.py). Auth already accepts API keys + passwords in git_backend._check_basic_auth.

title: Build Karpathy import tool: auto-generate log.md + schema.md from imported wikis
description:
When importing external content (blogs, Google Sites, GitHub Pages) into WikiHub, automatically generate:

1. **log.md** — retrospective timeline reconstructed from dates in filenames/content. Each dated entry gets a log line: '## 2017-02-01 — Added: Ways to Discover Contrarian Truths'. This creates the Karpathy activity feed pattern even for imported content.

2. **schema.md** — auto-generated from analyzing the content structure. Describes what the wiki covers, how it's organized, and instructions for an agent maintaining it.

3. **Ripple updates** — when a new raw source is ingested, the agent should update relevant wiki/ compiled pages AND append to log.md.

The key Karpathy mechanic: 'Ingesting one source can update 10-15 wiki pages.' The schema tells the agent HOW to do this. log.md records THAT it happened.

This should be a reusable tool/skill, not one-off code. Every wiki import should produce this structure automatically.

Reference: Karpathy gist https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f
See also: memory/reference_llmwiki.md for the three-layer pattern.

title: Batch-import Jacob's Google Docs exports into /@jacobcole/jacobcole
description:
## ⚠️ REVIEW THIS PLAN BEFORE EXECUTING

Jacob exported ~253 markdown files from his Google Docs universe to
`/Users/jacobcole/code/DataExports/` (also in `~/Documents/vibe-coding-guide/DataExports/`
and `~/Documents/Personal/Book-Draft/source-material/google-docs-full/`).

Only 22 pages made it to prod (`/@jacobcole/jacobcole`). **Import never ran at scale.**

## Export inventory
- `markdown-by-hierarchy/` — 88 files, canonical tree structure (chronicpain/, foodslist/, ideaflow/, infrastructure/, manifestos/, misc/, philosophy/, thingsyoudidntknowexisted/, worldgestalts/, worldquestguild/)
- `markdown-by-category/` — 89 files, same docs re-grouped (duplicate of hierarchy)
- `markdown-new-missing-docs/` — 48 files, second-pass export catching what first pass missed
- `markdown-new-subdomains/` — 26 files, each satellite site (sleep/aspirations/lifehacks/healingartsgrant/etc.) as its own doc

After dedup: ~120 unique documents to import. Current: 22. Gap: ~100.

## Proposed plan (REVIEW)

**Target:** `/@jacobcole/jacobcole` (personal wiki)

**Steps:**
1. Write a batch importer script (`scripts/import_jacobcole_gdocs.py`) that walks a source tree and uploads to `POST /api/v1/wikis/jacobcole/jacobcole/pages`, preserving relative paths.
2. **Dry-run first.** Print every page it *would* create with size + path. No writes.
3. Sources in order (with dedup on normalized slug):
    - `markdown-by-hierarchy/` (primary, canonical tree)
    - `markdown-new-missing-docs/` (layer on, under `missing/` prefix OR same tree if paths match)
    - `markdown-new-subdomains/` (under `subdomains/` prefix — each satellite site as its own page)
    - **SKIP** `markdown-by-category/` (duplicate of hierarchy)
4. **Default visibility: `private`** — safer for a maximal personal brain dump. Jacob flips pages `public` selectively afterward.
5. **Conflict policy:** skip existing pages by default (don't clobber the 22 curated ones). Optional `--force` flag to overwrite.
6. Post-import: run a PII/privacy scanner on imported pages that lists any page containing:
    - Email addresses (`[\w\.]+@[\w\.-]+`)
    - Phone numbers (`\d{3}[- .]?\d{3}[- .]?\d{4}`)
    - Google Docs URLs (`docs\.google\.com/document/d/\S+`)
    - URLs to other private-ish satellite sites (`\*\.jacobcole\.net`, `\*\.jacobblog\.net`)
   So Jacob can review before flipping anything public.

## Flags to review explicitly before making public
These docs likely contain other people's info:
- `hiringblurb.md`, `hiringlist.md`, `hotconnections.md`, `housinglist.md`, `ilparty.md`
- `shadirecs.md` (shadi recommendations)
- `people/*` (if any)
- `productfeedback/bugslist.md`, `productfeedback/productfeedback.md` (internal ideaflow)

## Out of scope for this ticket
- Recursively fetching the satellite subdomain docs fresh from the web (already in `markdown-new-subdomains/` from the export)
- Breaking into multiple wikis (e.g. `/@jacobcole/health-wellness`). Can be done later by moving pages.
- Visibility flips — Jacob reviews the PII report and flips manually.

## Backup before execution
Prod backup from 2026-04-20 already exists at `~/wikihub-backups/prod-2026-04-20-020552/` (db dump + app tar + nginx + SSL). Good safety net if import misbehaves.

title: Review: Editorial Picks on Explore — how should curation work?
description:
Currently Editorial Picks on /explore is hardcoded to show @wikihub/wiki as a single wiki card. This was auto-generated code, not a deliberate product decision.

Options:
1. **Current: hardcoded @wikihub/wiki card** — just shows the official wiki. Not really 'editorial picks.'
2. **Pages from @wikihub/wiki as picks** — the Featured Wikis page we created has curated links. Could render those as cards instead of showing the wiki container.
3. **Tag/flag system** — wikis get an 'editorial_pick' flag, admins toggle it. Explore queries for flagged wikis.
4. **Curated from the community index wiki** — @wikihub/wikihub Featured Wikis page is the source of truth. A special tag or frontmatter field marks wikis for the explore page.
5. **Remove editorial section entirely** — just show popular (by stars) and recent. Let the @wikihub wiki be the editorial venue, not the explore page.

The community index wiki (@wikihub/wikihub/Featured Wikis) already exists as editorial curation. The question is whether /explore should also have its own editorial section or just link to the community wiki.

title: Editor preview tab shows plaintext instead of rendered markdown
description: The Preview tab in the editor (editor.html) shows raw markdown text via textContent, not rendered output. Needs either a client-side markdown renderer or an AJAX call to the server's render endpoint. Related to wikihub-18e (editor QA). This is the biggest UX gap in the editing experience.

title: D3 force-graph of wikilinks
description: Wikilink model already tracks [[link]] refs between pages. Need: (1) API endpoint to dump graph as JSON (nodes + edges), (2) page with D3 force layout visualization. ~80 lines of D3. High demo value.

title: DM / messaging between users — Twitter-style integrated chat
description: Agent-friendly messaging: 'send Harrison a link to my orchestrator skill'. Should feel integrated like Twitter DMs — not a separate app. Consider overlap with existing OpenChat project (~/code/open-chat or similar). Options: (1) build into wikihub directly, (2) integrate OpenChat as a module, (3) separate service with deep wikihub links. Key use case: sharing wiki pages/content between users with context. Future: agent-to-agent messaging for orchestration across users.

title: Add comment-only access tier for collaboration
description: Consider a distinct access level between read and write: comment access. Users with this role should be able to leave page-level or inline comments without being able to edit the page directly. Scope: permission model, UI affordances on the reader page, owner moderation/review workflow, interaction with future suggestion flows, and whether comment access is page-level, wiki-level, or both.

title: Link-share token expiry
description: v1 link tokens are permanent. Add expiry syntax or Postgres-side override.

title: Show lock/sharing icon on sidebar pages based on visibility and grants
description: Sidebar page list should show visibility state per-page: lock icon for private, share/people icon for pages shared with specific users, link icon for unlisted, no icon for public. Currently the sidebar shows all pages identically — you can't tell at a glance which are private, shared, or public. This is important context when browsing someone else's wiki where you've been granted access to specific pages.

title: Add a dedicated meeting-notes template/scaffold
description:
Google Drive readiness assessment found that WikiHub has general structured/freeform scaffolds but no explicit meeting-notes template.

Desired behavior:
- add a meeting-notes scaffold that is easy for a small team to start using immediately
- should fit existing WikiHub patterns (markdown, wikilinks, optional action items / decisions / notes structure)
- available from the existing wiki/page creation flows without introducing heavy new machinery

Why:
Meeting notes are a core shared-workspace use case and one of the faster ways to narrow the Google Drive gap.

Evidence:
- /tmp/wikihub-gdrive-assessment.md
- app/templates/new_wiki.html
- app/git_sync.py scaffold/template plumbing

title: Volume backup strategy for git repos (AWS Lightsail)
description: Persistent volume at /data/repos holds all bare git repos. If Railway recycles or loses the volume, all wiki content is gone (DB has metadata but git content is lost). Need automated backup — e.g. periodic tar to S3/R2, or git bundle push to object storage.

title: Verify public-edit authoring and merge flow end to end
description:
Validate two user-critical collaboration flows end to end:

1. Publicly editable wikis/pages can actually be edited by non-owners and anonymous/public users where intended.
2. The merge flow works correctly from proposal/diff through merge application.

This is a verification task, not a product spec. It should capture:
- exact flows tested
- environments tested (local/prod if possible)
- failures found
- follow-up bug tickets if either flow is broken

Requested explicitly by the user while preparing the IdeaFlow team wiki import.
notes: Production public-edit verification passed on 2026-04-22 using @jacobcole/public-edit-verification/wiki/open: anonymous API edit succeeded and unauthenticated web UI showed the Edit button. Merge-flow verification is blocked by the apparent absence of an implemented merge/proposal surface in the current repo; follow-up ticket created.

title: Sort accounts and wikis by recent activity on Explore page
Sidebar: sort pages by date (default) instead of alphabetical
Hover preview: cap stacking depth to match LessWrong (+1 as potential bump)
description:
Follow-up to wikihub-5613 stack-leak fixes. Current behavior: unlimited recursion on related-link hovers (no depth cap).

**Goal:** match LessWrong's hover-preview depth behavior (that's the inspiration this feature is cloning). In my previous recommendation I said cap=3 on the assumption LessWrong stops around there in practice — **verify the actual cap LessWrong uses before implementing**, and ship that number, not a guess.

**The +1 question:** once we know LessWrong's cap N, consider shipping N+1 instead so users can explore 'one more step' than the reference. Trade-off:

Arguments for matching LessWrong exactly (N):
- clean design-reference match
- proven by their scale
- beyond N, users are probably better served by clicking through to a full page

Arguments for N+1:
- users who want to explore a concept neighborhood one more step get it
- still manageable on most laptops with alternating below/above placement
- with current fixes (timer leaks fixed, mouseleave suppression narrowed, orphan sweep, click-outside always closes), the reliability cost of one more level is low

**Implementation regardless:** one-line change in the related-link mouseenter handler — early-return when (depth + 1) is greater than or equal to CAP. The link keeps its href and normal click still navigates. Add an e2e test that POSTs a page with deeply-nested wikilinks and verifies the Nth hover-preview-link's mouseenter does not spawn an (N+1)th card.

See also: wikihub-5613 (the stack-leak fixes) — that work fixed reliability but did not introduce any cap.

title: Session cookie missing Secure and SameSite flags
description: Session cookie is set with only HttpOnly; Path=/. Missing: Secure flag (required for HTTPS to prevent transmission over HTTP), SameSite=Lax or Strict (CSRF prevention). Fix: set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True and SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' in Flask config.

title: Clean up junk/test accounts polluting production People page
description: Multiple test accounts are visible on production People page: stress-test-1, stresstest123, stresstest_probe_99, ratelimit-test-1 through ratelimit-test-5, 'test spaces', 'test<script>...', test_upper, '../../../etc/passwd'. Need DB cleanup and possibly an admin delete-account endpoint.

title: Signup: auto-derive username from email (keep required but prefilled)
description:
Today `/auth/signup` requires a username with no hint that the site could pick one for you. For invite-flow signups the email is already prefilled; the username should be too.

## Proposed behavior

- When the email field has a value (prefilled via invite link, or typed by the user), sanitize the local-part into a candidate username and prefill the username field.
- Collision resolution: if the candidate is taken, append a counter (`tejas`, `tejas2`, `tejas3`) — same logic as `google_callback` at `app/routes/auth.py:272-280`.
- As the user edits the email, the username prefill updates live (debounced, via a small JS listener) unless the user has already edited the username field manually.
- Username stays REQUIRED — zero-input silent generation produces ugly URL slugs (`/@user_abc123`) that users will want to rename, creating username-rename support pain.

## Why not optional

WikiHub usernames are URL slugs (`wikihub.md/@tejas`). Users care about them. They should see what they're getting. Auto-generated `user_abc123` would be a worse UX than a 3-second typed username.

## Extract shared helper

The email → username sanitization logic already exists inline in `google_callback` (`app/routes/auth.py:266-280`). Extract to a helper in `app/auth_utils.py`: `suggest_username_from_email(email: str) -> str`. Use from:
- `auth.google_callback` (existing)
- `auth.signup` GET renderer (new — pass as `suggested_username` template var)
- Hypothetical future /api/v1/signup-preview endpoint (if someone wants autocomplete UX)

## Discovered via

Invite-flow user testing 2026-04-22. Currently a teammate has to think up a username on the spot when an invite link lands them on signup; prefill removes that cognitive load.

title: JSON-LD structured data on pages
description: Add <script type=application/ld+json> block to page template with @type: Article, title, author, dateModified. Trivial effort, free SEO/discoverability.

title: Epic: GitHub URL → instant LLM wiki (paste-to-view, with optional pushback)
description:
Status: NEEDS JACOB REVIEW before implementation. Full plan captured below.

## Core concept

Treat a WikiHub URL like `/gh/<owner>/<repo>` as a **cacheable, editable "view" on a GitHub repo**. Read-through on first hit, editable after. Pushback to GitHub is a later phase, not v1.

Framing: "paste GitHub URL → instant LLM wiki." A key acquisition path — most users already have knowledge in public repos and should see WikiHub value without manual page creation.

## Related existing tickets

- wikihub-0cxg (P1) — Onboarding: paste public GitHub URL → generated wiki  → matches Phase 1
- wikihub-40tr (P1) — GitHub↔WikiHub sync + pushback (PR vs direct push) → matches Phases 4 + 5
- wikihub-chil (P1) — Private repo import with selective exposure → matches Phase 6
- wikihub-ibt.2 (P2) — URL/repo connect plumbing. NOTE: ticket says "plumbing exists in git_sync.py" but that is WRONG — git_sync.py manages internal bare repos only, not external clone. External `git clone` still needs to be written.
- wikihub-ibt.1 (P2) — Paste unstructured text → LLM-generated wiki → reusable for Phase 2

## Phasing

| Phase | Scope | Maps to | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. MVP paste-URL | Form on landing page + `POST /import/github`. Shallow-clone → filter text/md files → copy into new wiki bare repo via existing `apply_repo_changes`. Lands at `/@<you>/<repo>`. | wikihub-0cxg | 1–2 days |
| 2. LLM-generated structure | After clone, Claude pass generates index.md + overview / key-files / getting-started pages. Platform key, budget-capped. | wikihub-ibt.1 (adapted) | 3–5 days |
| 3. URL endpoint | `/gh/<owner>/<repo>` — first hit clones and redirects, later hits serve cached. Imports land under `@<gh-owner>` as `claimable=True`, source-stamped. Real GitHub owner can later claim via OAuth. | NEW TICKET NEEDED | 2 days |
| 4. Refresh from GitHub | "Synced 6h ago · refresh" button. Fetch → diff → apply non-conflicting upstream changes. | wikihub-40tr (one-way half) | 3 days |
| 5. Pushback | "PR this upstream" on each page. Requires GitHub OAuth. | wikihub-40tr (bidi half) | 1 week |
| 6. Private import | GitHub App for private repos + selective public exposure via `.wikihub/export.md`. | wikihub-chil | separate design |

## URL-endpoint design (Phase 3) — options

Option A: **`/gh/<owner>/<repo>`** — short, lowercase, distinct from `@` user namespace. Jacob/Claude preference.
Option B: **`/github.com/<owner>/<repo>`** — mirrors DeepWiki; self-explanatory. Downside: `github.com` as a path segment is weird.
Option C: **No separate route** — just `/@<owner>/<repo>` with `source=github` flag, relying on the synthetic-account namespace.

First-visit behavior:
1. Check if `@<owner>/<repo>` wiki exists → redirect.
2. If not: enqueue clone, show "importing…" page with live status, redirect when ready. (Synchronous clone won't work for big repos — Phase 1.5 needs a background worker.)
3. Imported wiki owned by synthetic `@<owner>` account marked `claimable=True, source=github:<owner>/<repo>`. Real GitHub owner claims later via OAuth (prove `owner` == GitHub login).

## Open questions (blocking decisions)

1. URL pattern: A (`/gh/...`), B (`/github.com/...`), or C (no separate route)?
2. Is Phase 1 MVP OK as "dumb mirror of markdown files" with no LLM restructure — or is the LLM pass (Phase 2) table-stakes for the first paste-URL demo?
3. Pushback (Phase 5) — PR model or direct-push? PR is safer but needs OAuth from day one.

## Risks / design details flagged

- Clone timeouts for big repos — need background worker by Phase 1.5.
- Rate-limiting abuse — anyone can trigger a clone of anything. Need IP/global rate limit.
- License/attribution — auto-extract LICENSE into sidebar, stamp source URL on every page.
- Namespace collisions — `@mitchellh` on WikiHub ≠ GitHub's mitchellh. Synthetic-account namespace `github:<owner>` vs user-facing `@<owner>`.
- Size caps for Phase 1: < 100MB, < 5k files.
- File filter: skip `.git`, `node_modules`, binaries > 1MB, lockfiles.

## Next steps (after Jacob reviews)

1. Jacob answers the 3 open questions.
2. Create 2 new sub-tickets (URL endpoint, LLM restructure) and link wikihub-0cxg / 40tr / chil / ibt.1 / ibt.2 as children of this epic.
3. Start Phase 1 MVP.

title: Curator frontend: slide-out chat panel on reader pages
description:
Build the frontend chat panel for the Curator agent. Backend already exists at /api/v1/agent/chat (SSE endpoint in app/routes/agent_chat.py).

## UI

title: Agents over-create wikis; should favor pages/folders in existing wikis
description:
## Observation

Agents (concrete example: Open Asks work on 2026-04-18, session creating /@jacobcole/asks) default to creating a new wiki when asked for a "section" about a topic. A wiki in WikiHub is a full git repository — bare repo + public mirror + ACL + audit log + sidebar entry on the user profile. That is heavy. A page of open asks does not justify a separate repo.

## Correct default

Create a page (or folder of pages) within an existing wiki. Reach for a new wiki only when:

- The content is a distinct, standalone body of work with its own identity and lifecycle
- It will plausibly be forked, starred, or cloned as a unit
- It has a distinct collaborator set (different ACL)
- It is a wiki *about* a topic, not a page *on* a topic

For Jacob's use case, "Open Asks" is a page; "admitsphere" or "rsi-wiki" are wikis.

## Contrast with Noos

Noos list nodes are the opposite: cheap, encouraged to proliferate, one per organizational concept. The graph absorbs the sprawl because edges do the work. Agents should pattern-match to the grain of each system — page-first in WikiHub, list-freely in Noos. See the paired Noos issue for the philosophical half of this.

## Action items

- [ ] Update AGENTS.md: add a "when to create a new wiki vs. a page" section with the heuristic above
- [ ] Consider surfacing folder-based organization more prominently in the UI (page tree sidebar exists; the create-page UX could nudge toward `folder/page.md` paths)
- [ ] Migrate /@jacobcole/asks content to a single page inside an existing wiki (probably thoughtstream/asks.md); delete the standalone asks wiki once content is moved and redirects (if any) are in place
- [ ] Decide whether llms.txt / AGENTS.md route guidance should include this heuristic so connected agents pick it up

## Related

- Noos twin of this ticket (philosophical reference): to be created
- Concrete artifact of the mistake: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/asks (scheduled for migration)

title: Password reset flow: /auth/forgot and /auth/reset/<token>
description: Add `PasswordResetToken(id, user_id, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)`, 30-min TTL, single-use. POST /auth/forgot {email}: always returns 204 (no enumeration). If a verified email matches a user with password_hash set → mint token + email reset link. If matched user is Google-only → email says 'your account uses Google Sign-In, sign in with Google'. No match → silent no-op. GET /auth/reset/<token> → form. POST /auth/reset/<token> → verify token (unused + unexpired), update password_hash, mark token used, invalidate all other reset tokens for that user, bump session_version (see #6) to kick other sessions. Rate limit: 5/hr per email, 20/hr per IP (deque pattern like login). Requires email-sending + verification infra.

title: Cmd+K trigger looks bad on homepage
description: The Cmd+K trigger element in the top-left nav looks visually broken or out of place on the homepage/landing page. Needs styling fix to match the rest of the nav.

title: Zero-shot onboarding worked example in /AGENTS.md
description:
Users want to paste a prompt into Claude Code (or any coding agent) that makes the agent do: signup with email → create a private wiki → invite teammates by email. Today /AGENTS.md covers individual endpoints but has no end-to-end worked example, so the agent does a lot of discovery and back-and-forth.

Fix: add an 'Onboarding in one call' (or similar) section near the top of /AGENTS.md with a copy-paste shell script that chains the three steps using only documented endpoints:
- POST /api/v1/accounts with {username, email} → captures api_key
- POST /api/v1/wikis with {slug, title}  (private is the default — the scaffolded .wikihub/acl starts with * private)
- POST /api/v1/wikis/<user>/<slug>/share/bulk with grants=[{email, role: 'edit'}, ...]
  - bulk-share already handles unknown emails by stashing pending invites

Also update the rendered /agents HTML template (per the 'agent instruction surfaces' sync rule in dev AGENTS.md) so humans see the same recipe.

Deliverable: pasting 'read https://wikihub.md/AGENTS.md, then create an account with <my_email>, a private wiki called <name>, and share it with <teammate_emails>' to Claude Code / Cursor / Codex should complete in one round-trip per step, no guessing.

title: /settings: change password (with session invalidation), change email, linked accounts, link/unlink Google
description: Add to /settings: (1) Change password — requires current password, updates password_hash, bumps session_version on User (new column) to force re-login elsewhere (Flask-Login doesn't track sessions server-side, so we check session_version on each request). (2) Change email — kicks off verification flow on new address. (3) Linked accounts panel — shows Google linkage; 'Link Google Account' launches OAuth with state=link&user_id=me so callback adds google_id to current user instead of creating a new one; 'Unlink Google' allowed only if password_hash is set (else user would be locked out). Add session_version column to User; include in session payload; reject sessions whose version mismatches current.

title: Regression: binary file serving — Page.visibility=public anon access returns 404
description:
test_binary_file_serving fails on cli-installer branch with:

`Page.visibility=public should grant anon access, got 404`

Pre-existing on the branch (reproduced via `git stash` of wikihub-skp7/exj1 work — still fails). Anonymous access to binary files whose Page.visibility is 'public' used to work. Something on this branch regressed it.

Likely suspects: recent changes to public-mirror regeneration, ACL visibility resolution, or the binary file serving route in app/routes/wiki.py.

Reproduce: `source .venv/bin/activate && python3 tests/test_e2e.py` on this branch.

Not blocking wikihub-skp7/exj1 deploy — the sharing and pending-invite flows are orthogonal to binary assets.

title: Bulk + individual collaborator add (CLI + UI)
description:
Add teammates to a wiki by username, email, or in bulk. Covers:

**CLI**
- `wikihub-cli share add <wiki> <user|email> [<user|email>...] --role=read|edit` — accept multiple identifiers in one call
- `wikihub-cli share ls <wiki>` — list current grants
- `wikihub-cli share rm <wiki> <user|email> [...]` — bulk revoke

**Web UI**
- Collaborators section on wiki settings page (app/templates/wiki_settings.html)
- Multi-token input with autocomplete on username (and maybe display name)
- Paste-friendly: accept comma/newline-separated list of emails/usernames
- Inline role picker (read/edit) per grant
- Shows current grants with revoke button

**API**
- `POST /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/share/bulk` accepting `{grants: [{pattern, username|email, role}, ...]}`
- `GET /api/v1/users/search?q=<prefix>` for autocomplete (public usernames only)
- Existing single-grant endpoint stays for backward compat

**Open questions**
- Invite-by-email for users who don't have accounts yet? (Deferred — out of scope for v1; reject unknown emails with clear error.)
- Wiki-level grant vs path-pattern grant? (UI defaults to wiki-level pattern `**`; power users can still use CLI for patterns.)

Closes part of wikihub-fd4 (per-person sharing settings page).

title: Signup: username auto-derive from email local-part (JS live prefill)
description:
When user types into the email field on /auth/signup, auto-populate the username field with the sanitized local-part (e.g. tejas@ideaflow.io → tejas). Stop auto-updating once user manually edits username (JS dirty flag). Reuse the sanitization logic from google_callback (strip non-alphanumeric, enforce 2-40 chars). This is the follow-up to the field reorder — username prefill makes the form feel like one-field signup.

Related: wikihub-3i9i (filed earlier with same scope). Consider consolidating.

title: Electron app + offline mode — potential Obsidian replacement
Serve binary files from git repo (images 404)
description:
![[image.png]] Obsidian embeds are supported in the renderer but there's no route to actually serve binary files from the git repo. Images referenced in markdown pages 404.

Needs a route that serves raw files from the authoritative or public mirror repo via git cat-file, with proper content-type detection and cache headers. Should respect the same auth dispatch as other wiki reads (owner gets authoritative repo, everyone else gets public mirror).

This is Stage 0 of the file support roadmap (wikihub-291) — prerequisite for any image/attachment functionality to work.

title: Reader page-share modal mishandles email invite responses
description:
Workflow audit finding from 2026-04-22.

The page-level share flow in `reader.html` assumes a `grant`-style response, but the API returns `{invited: email, pattern, role}` for unknown emails. Result: inviting by email from the reader UI is inconsistent/broken even though the backend accepts it.

Likely fix:
- update the page-share modal JS to handle pending-invite responses cleanly
- or normalize the backend response shape for page-share invites
- add an e2e/browser regression for inviting an unknown email from the page share UI

Evidence:
- app/templates/reader.html page-share JS
- app/routes/api_wikis.py invite response path
- workflow audit report /tmp/wikihub-workflow-audit.md
notes: Claimed from workflow audit follow-through on 2026-04-22. Fixing reader page-share invite response handling.

title: Render .txt and plain-text files in browser (currently 404)
description:
WikiHub's web reader (app/routes/wiki.py:822-847) handles markdown via the page route and a fixed list of "binary" extensions (.png, .pdf, .csv, .json, etc.) via a raw-bytes path. .txt files fall through neither and 404 in the browser, even though they're in the repo and accessible via API/git clone.

Concrete failure: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/vision-convo/raw/transcript-original.txt returns 404 in browser.

Minimal fix (this ticket): add .txt and .log to _BINARY_EXTS so they serve as text/plain via mimetypes. Browser displays inline.

Larger follow-up (separate ticket if wanted): wrap text files in the page chrome (sidebar + breadcrumb + theme) with content in a <pre> block, like a poor-man's syntax viewer. Better UX for navigation but more invasive — leaves the question of how to handle frontmatter, links, etc. in non-md files.

title: Search leaks ACL-shared private pages to unrelated authenticated users
Editor and new-page experience is broken — needs full QA pass
description:
The entire editing-a-page-manually and creating-a-new-page web experience is borked. This is P0 — the editor is a core user flow.

Needs investigation:
- Can you create a new page from the web UI? Does the form work?
- Can you edit an existing page? Does the editor load? Does save work?
- Is the Milkdown editor bundled and loading correctly?
- Are there JS errors in the console?
- Does the raw textarea fallback work if Milkdown isn't loaded?
- Does visibility setting work when creating/editing?
- Does the breadcrumb/navigation work after save?

This blocks dogfooding — if you can't manually edit pages, the site is API-only.

title: Recurring sidebar indentation regression on nested items with visibility icons
description: The left-hand sidebar indentation is regressing again on nested content. In the reported case, child pages under a folder do not read as clearly nested, especially when visibility icons are present on the child rows. This needs a deeper audit of the layout invariants instead of another one-off pixel tweak. Scope: folder toggle width, child padding, icon alignment, active-row background, rows with trailing visibility icons, and any divergence between server-rendered and async sidebar markup. Treat this as a recurring regression in the same family as wikihub-58c and wikihub-58bd.

title: download button: clarify wiki-level vs page-level download
description: when viewing a single page (not wiki root), the download button offers 'Download ZIP' and 'Copy clone URL' but these download the ENTIRE wiki, not just the current page. this is confusing. fix: (1) make the dropdown text explicit — e.g. 'Download wiki as ZIP' and 'Clone entire wiki'. (2) add a 'Download this page (.md)' option that downloads just the current page's markdown file. the page-level download is just a link to the .md content-negotiated URL.

title: Stub wikilinks: visual treatment in hover previews (and generally)
description:
## Problem

Stub wikilinks (pointing to articles that do not exist yet) need a clearer visual treatment — both inline in content and inside hover previews. Same goes for links to inaccessible/private articles the viewer cannot read. Right now they are visually indistinct from normal links, which is confusing in hover popups especially.

## Questions to resolve

- Should stubs get a distinct color (dim / red / dashed underline)?
- Same or different treatment for: (a) page does not exist, (b) page exists but the viewer lacks permission?
- In hover previews, if a stub is hovered, what should the card show — "click to create" vs "private page (no access)"?
- Apply the same treatment in the reader (body wikilinks), sidebar, and hover cards — or only hovers?

## Scope

Mostly CSS + minor renderer logic in:
- app/renderer.py (wikilink class attr)
- app/templates/reader.html (hover card markup + stub body)
- /preview endpoint if it differentiates missing vs private

## Related

- Recent hover work: 414815d, 3fca08f
- User report (2026-04-20): "stubs need to show up and hover... I don't know if they should get a special color or something both there and in general, but especially in the hover previews."

title: D3 force-graph render of wikilinks (site-wide)
description: Cross-wiki graph visualization. Extends the per-wiki v1.5 graph to full platform view.

title: Real-time collaborative editing
description: CRDT/OT. Massive undertaking. Async-first, not biased toward realtime.

title: Magic login links — exchange API key for one-time browser login URL
description:
Add endpoint that an agent (with API key) can call to generate a short-lived, one-time-use URL that logs the user into the browser without leaking the API key.

Flow:
1. Agent: POST /api/v1/magic-link (Bearer API key) → returns {url, expires_at}
2. User clicks URL (e.g. https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/auth/magic/<token>)
3. Server validates token, sets session cookie, redirects to / (or ?next=)
4. Token marked used_at; cannot be reused

Design:
- New MagicLink model: id, user_id, token_hash (SHA-256), expires_at, used_at, created_at
- Token: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) → only hash stored in DB
- TTL: 10 minutes default, overrideable via ttl_seconds param (max 60 min)
- On consume: atomic check+mark used_at; constant-time compare
- Audit log entry on both creation and consumption
- Rate limit creation (e.g. 10/hour per user) — MVP can skip
- Update all agent surfaces (AGENTS.md, /AGENTS.md route, /llms.txt, MCP tools)
- e2e test: create link, GET /auth/magic/<token>, verify session cookie set, second GET returns 410

title: wiki homepage with categories, stats, and featured article
description: render a rich wiki homepage from frontmatter tags instead of a flat folder listing. group pages by type/category tag, show article count and category count, featured article section, browse by category grid. inspired by Farzapedia's '417 articles across 41 categories' homepage.

title: File support roadmap: from image embeds to Google Drive mode
description:
Progressive file support beyond markdown-only browsing. The spec already covers storing binaries in git repos and rendering Obsidian ![[image.png]] embeds, but there's no serving route (images 404) and the sidebar/UI is markdown-only.

**Progressive stages (each unlocks the next):**

Stage 0 — Serve binaries from git repo (bug fix, not feature)
- Route to serve raw files from authoritative/public repo via git cat-file
- Content-type detection, cache headers
- Prerequisite for ![[image.png]] embeds to actually render

Stage 1 — Image attachments (core LLMWiki need)
- Upload images alongside markdown pages
- Inline image preview in editor
- Image gallery or attachment panel per page
- LLM wikis need diagrams, charts, screenshots — this is table stakes

Stage 2 — File browser
- Show all files in sidebar/tree, not just .md
- File type icons
- Non-markdown files visible but clearly secondary to wiki pages

Stage 3 — Non-markdown preview
- Inline PDF viewer, CSV table render, image lightbox
- Reference material browsable alongside wiki pages

Stage 4 — Google Drive mode (future, maybe separate product)
- Drag-drop any file type, bulk upload
- Full-text search across non-markdown files
- Folder-first browsing (vs page-first)
- This diverges from LLMWiki core mission — needs careful product thinking

**Key question:** How far past Stage 1 should we go before it stops being an LLMWiki and starts being a document hosting platform? The Karpathy pattern is markdown knowledge bases maintained by LLMs — images support that, but a Google Drive clone doesn't.

**ListHub contrast:** ListHub is deliberately text-only (flat markdown items, no binary support). WikiHub already went further architecturally (git repos can hold anything), but the serving/browsing layer is markdown-only. The gap is between what git stores and what the UI shows.

**Spec reference:** Binary storage is already specced (wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md, Data Architecture section). This epic covers the serving, browsing, and UX layers on top.

description: Deferred ACL modes: signed-in, link-share, link:token:role grants, group:name:role grants.

title: GET /api/v1/wikis returns 405 — no list-wikis endpoint
description: The /api/v1/wikis route only supports POST (create wiki). There is no GET handler to list all wikis. The llms.txt and AGENTS.md suggest agents should be able to discover wikis, but the only way is via /api/v1/search or the /explore web page. Either add a GET /api/v1/wikis endpoint or update docs.

title: Add browser-driven core web workflow smoke suite
description: Current e2e coverage is strong at the Flask/client level, but we still lack a committed browser-driven happy-path smoke that exercises the real web UI for owner login, wiki creation, folder/page creation, editing, sharing, and collaborator read/write access. Add a lightweight maintained smoke path so UI regressions are caught before deploy.

title: New page button is in WIKIS section, should be next to pages heading
Incremental public mirror update instead of full rebuild on every page write
description: Every page create/update/delete calls regenerate_public_mirror which rebuilds the entire mirror from scratch (ls-tree all files, cat-file each, hash-object each, update-index each). For a wiki with N pages, that's ~3N git subprocess calls. Add an incremental update_mirror_page() that loads the existing mirror tree with read-tree, then adds/updates/removes just the one changed file. Full rebuild stays for ACL changes, bulk ops, renames.

title: IdeaFlow Notion import cleanup: strip block-type markers and linkify child pages
description:
IdeaFlow Notion import markdown currently leaks raw Notion block types into the published content, e.g. literal lines like `[paragraph]` and `[child_page]` in the seed repo and live imported wiki.

Need to:
- identify the exporter/transform step emitting those markers
- render paragraph blocks as plain markdown text instead of prefixed block labels
- render child_page blocks as actual links using the child title and, when available, a resolvable target path or source Notion URL
- refresh the generated seed/import content after the transform is fixed

Examples observed on 2026-04-22 in the IdeaFlow import include `vision/explaining-ideaflow-2024.md`, `roadmap/feature-roadmap.md`, and `team/onboarding-quickstart-guide.md`.

title: Sidebar: collapsible folders that auto-expand to current page
description:
## Problem

When navigating directly to a deep page like /@wikihub/wikihub/philosophy/The Librarian, the sidebar shows the folder tree but:
1. Folders aren't collapsible — the full tree is always expanded or always collapsed
2. When landing on a nested page, the relevant folder isn't auto-opened to show where you are

## Expected behavior

- Sidebar folders should be collapsible (click to toggle open/closed)
- When navigating directly to a page, the folder containing that page should auto-expand
- Other folders should default to collapsed, keeping the sidebar clean
- The current page should be visually highlighted and scrolled into view

## Files involved

- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro + JS for toggle
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same sidebar rendering
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` already sets `active` flag on items

## Notes

The toggle JS (`toggleFolder()`) and CSS (`.sidebar-folder.open`) already exist. The issue is that the default open/closed state isn't intelligently set based on the current page path.

Research Reflect.app folder/page creation behavior for UX reference
move TOC and backlinks to right sidebar instead of inline
description: currently TOC is at the top and backlinks at the bottom of the article, pushing content down and leaving empty space on the right. consider a right sidebar layout: TOC + backlinks (and later, wikilink graph) in a sticky right column, with a draggable border between the content area and right sidebar. this is a layout-sensitive change — the current two-column layout (left sidebar nav + content) would become three-column (left nav + article + right panel). IMPORTANT: this needs extensive testing on the dev server (localhost:5100) before pushing to production. test at multiple viewport widths, test on pages with no TOC (< 3 headings), test with and without backlinks, test mobile collapse behavior. do NOT push to prod without agent-browser verification at each breakpoint.

title: No security headers (X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS)
description: The application serves no security headers — no X-Frame-Options, no Content-Security-Policy, no Strict-Transport-Security, no X-Content-Type-Options. The site can be iframed by any domain (clickjacking risk) and there is no CSP to mitigate XSS.

title: Signup form not detected by password managers (1Password)
description:
## Problem

The signup / create account page is not recognized by 1Password (and likely other password managers) as an account creation form. 1Password does not offer to autofill or save credentials.

## Likely cause

Password managers detect signup forms via:
- `autocomplete="new-password"` on the password field
- `autocomplete="username"` or `autocomplete="email"` on the identity field
- `<form>` element wrapping the inputs
- Input `name` and `type` attributes (`type="email"`, `type="password"`, `name="email"`)

If any of these are missing or non-standard (e.g., using `<div>` instead of `<form>`, custom input components without proper attributes, or missing `autocomplete` hints), password managers won't detect it.

## Fix

Ensure the signup form has:
1. A `<form>` element wrapping all inputs
2. `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` for the email field
3. `<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="new-password">` for the password field
4. `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` for the username/display name field
5. A `<button type="submit">` (not just an onclick handler)

Test with 1Password browser extension after fixing — it should offer to save new credentials on submit.

title: backlinks: surface 'what links here' from wikilinks table
description: surface 'what links here' backlinks in the page sidebar. data already exists in the wikilinks table — just need to query and render. like Wikipedia's 'what links here' feature.

title: Improve default profile text for users without bios
Rename visibility levels for clarity: public→public-view, unlisted→unlisted-view
description:
## Problem

The current visibility names are confusing. 'public' doesn't make it clear that it means read-only (no editing). Users could reasonably think 'public' means anyone can edit.

## Proposed Naming

| Current | New | Meaning |
|---------|-----|---------|
| public | public-view | Anyone can read, only owner can edit |
| public-edit | public-edit | Anyone can read AND edit |
| unlisted | unlisted-view | Only people with the link can read |
| private | private | Only owner/ACL can read |

## Scope

- Update the `VALID_VISIBILITIES` set in `app/acl.py`
- Update all references in routes, templates, models
- Update frontmatter parsing to accept both old and new names (backwards compat)
- Update the visibility badge labels in reader.html, folder.html
- Update API docs
- Migration: existing pages with 'public' should map to 'public-view', 'unlisted' to 'unlisted-view'
- Keep accepting old names in frontmatter for backwards compatibility (Postel's Law)

## Files involved
- `app/acl.py` — VALID_VISIBILITIES
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py` — visibility endpoints
- `app/templates/reader.html` — visibility badges
- `app/templates/folder.html` — visibility badges
- `app/models.py` — any visibility defaults
- `wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md` — update spec

## Notes
Candidate for Codex — thorough rename across codebase.

Explore: show recently updated wikis
description: Add a way on Explore to browse recently updated wikis, not just featured/top-starred content. Optionally consider recently active people as a later extension, but keep the initial implementation focused on recently updated wikis and a clear recency signal.

title: Collaborator sidebar.json drops ACL-shared private content on large wikis
description:
Workflow audit finding from 2026-04-22.

For large wikis, the UI fetches `sidebar.json` asynchronously. That endpoint currently uses public-repo mode for non-owners, so collaborators with ACL access can lose private shared navigation in the sidebar tree.

Likely fix:
- make `sidebar_json()` go through `_repo_access()`
- pass the current ACL user into `_build_sidebar_tree()` so collaborators see the correct filtered tree
- add regression coverage for collaborator navigation on large ACL-protected wikis

Evidence:
- app/routes/wiki.py around large-wiki sidebar loading and sidebar_json
- app/templates/reader.html, folder.html, profile.html consumers
- workflow audit report /tmp/wikihub-workflow-audit.md

title: Homepage: show git push/remote instructions for CLI usage
MCP server endpoint (/mcp)
description: Discovery endpoints exist (.well-known/mcp/server-card.json, /.well-known/mcp) but the actual /mcp endpoint serving MCP protocol is missing. Tools: whoami, search, read_page, list_pages, create_page, update_page, append_section, delete_page, set_visibility, share, create_wiki, fork_wiki, commit_log. This is the whole thesis — wikihub is GitHub for LLM wikis.

title: Consistent footer across homepage and app pages
Make folder-first organization the default path in UX and docs
description:
## Motivation

WikiHub supports nested paths (`folder/page.md`) but doesn't surface folder-first organization as the default anywhere. Result: agents and users over-create wikis when a folder would do.

Paired with Noos philosophical reference: will link once created.

## Action items

- [ ] AGENTS.md: add a "when to create a new wiki vs. folder vs. page" heuristic. Default: page. Reach for folder when a concept accretes multiple pages. Reach for a new wiki only when the content has its own lifecycle (forkable, separate ACL, distinct identity).
- [ ] `/llms.txt` and `/AGENTS.md` route: echo the heuristic so connected agents internalize it on first contact.
- [ ] Landing page / create-wiki UX: copy nudge — "If you're adding a section to existing content, consider creating a page or folder first."
- [ ] Create-page UX: autocomplete existing folder paths; show a small tree of where you are.
- [ ] Verify page tree sidebar renders cleanly with 2–3 levels of nesting.

## Related

- wikihub-aihm — the original over-creation observation
- Concrete mistake: /@jacobcole/asks (pending migration decision)

Update the deploy instructions to the actual working host/path and remove the stale key reference so future deploys do not fail on a nonexistent SSH identity file.

title: Auto-save API key to ~/.wikihub/credentials.json on signup (agent-first)
description:
When a user signs up via POST /api/v1/accounts, the response should guide agents/CLI to save the returned API key to a standard well-known location (~/.wikihub/credentials.json) so future agent calls can read it without manual copy-paste.

Design:
- Credentials file: ~/.wikihub/credentials.json (mode 0600)
- Format: {"default": {"server": "...", "username": "...", "api_key": "wh_..."}}
- Server returns 'credentials_path' hint in signup response
- AGENTS.md (repo + /AGENTS.md route + llms.txt) documents the convention
- Optional: add a /docs/cli.md with curl + jq snippet agents can use
- Optional: add a tiny 'wikihub' shell wrapper for convenience

title: Sidebar: drag-and-drop reordering of pages and folders
Hover previews: show stub cards + close secondary on reentry to primary
description:
## Problem 1: Stubs flash 'Loading…' then vanish

Wikilinks to non-existent pages have class \`wikilink-broken\` (set by app/renderer.py:321). When hovered, the current code (app/templates/base.html:599) calls the API which returns 404 and the card silently disappears. User expects a dedicated stub card saying the page does not exist yet (and ideally linking to create it).

## Problem 2: Secondary card won't close when returning to primary

Current mouseleave handler in base.html:541-551 keeps a card alive if relatedTarget is any card in the stack. If user hovers card B (depth 1) then moves back to card A (depth 0), card B stays open because A is also in the stack. User expects: leaving card B (even to return to A) closes B.

## Fix plan

1. In the mouseover handler, detect \`.wikilink-broken\` links and open a stub-style card (no fetch, includes 'Stub — page not created' message).
2. Rework the card mouseleave handler to compare depths: if moving to a card at depth d' < my depth, run closeFromDepth(d' + 1). Only keep alive when moving to a deeper (or equal-depth) card.

## Scope

- app/templates/base.html only (lines ~398-653)
- Existing CSS at lines 243-261 can gain a \`.hover-preview.stub\` modifier for visual distinction
- Ties into wikihub-zmzk (broader stub styling)

title: Friendly git push error: 'owner-only, use web edit or fork'
description:
Today git_backend.receive_pack returns a blanket 401 for both (a) unauthed and (b) authed-but-not-owner. The second case should be a 403 with a helpful body so the user sees something like: 'Push rejected — only @OWNER can push to @OWNER/SLUG. You can edit via the web at https://wikihub.md/@OWNER/SLUG, or fork at https://wikihub.md/@OWNER/SLUG (then send a PR when that lands).'

This is the stopgap UX while we decide the real collab-write architecture in (long-term ticket). No ACL changes, no new push semantics — just differentiate 401 vs 403 and write a useful message.

Files: app/git_backend.py receive_pack + info_refs (for the service=git-receive-pack branch).

Test: attempt `git push` to a wiki you don't own — confirm a 403 with the helpful body, confirm owner push still works.
